proposals.title: Deconstructing the Theory of Mind Module: Neural Overlap of Mentalising with Narrative and Social Reasoning in Health and Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Theory of Mind (ToM) is often conceptualised as a distinct, domain-specific cognitive module, with dedicated neural substrates like the temporo-parietal junction (TPJ). However, this modular view struggles to explain the complex, context-dependent nature of social inference and its frequent disruption in conditions like schizophrenia, where symptoms involve a fragmented sense of self and intrusive thoughts about others. A more parsimonious account, aligned with Frith's prior work on predictive coding and interpersonal coordination, is that mentalising emerges from the dynamic interaction of more general cognitive processes for narrative construction and social reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that the neural substrates of explicit mentalising tasks will show extensive and task-dependent overlap with those engaged by non-mentalising narrative comprehension and general social contingency learning, and that this overlap will be aberrant in patients with schizophrenia, correlating with specific symptoms of alien control and delusions of reference.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-modal fMRI design with three carefully matched conditions: (1) a classic ToM task (e.g., false-belief stories), (2) a complex narrative reasoning task with physical/causal inferences but no mental states, and (3) a social contingency task based on the 'waiter's tray' paradigm, assessing non-mentalistic coordination expectations. Using multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and functional connectivity (psychophysiological interactions), we will test for shared neural patterns between conditions. We will compare healthy controls with a cohort of patients with schizophrenia, correlating neural pattern similarity with symptom scales for delusions and passivity experiences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical test of the modular ToM hypothesis, potentially demonstrating that mental state attribution is supported by a flexible network also used for broader social and narrative inference. By linking neural overlap abnormalities to specific symptoms in schizophrenia, it will offer a mechanistic framework for understanding how disruptions in general predictive social coding lead to the profound alterations in self and other experience characteristic of the disorder.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has compared ToM to other domains, our approach uniquely integrates Frith's work on interpersonal coordination and perceptual expectations to design tightly matched control tasks that isolate narrative and social contingency processing, providing a stronger test of neural specificity. Furthermore, directly testing this overlap model in schizophrenia, where the 'fractionation' of social cognition is clinically central, moves beyond theoretical debate to clinical relevance.

proposals.title: Causal necessity of the left medial frontal gyrus for theory of mind: A lesion and online inhibition study using interactive, non-verbal paradigms
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies consistently implicate the left medial frontal gyrus (lmFG) in theory of mind (ToM), but its causal necessity remains debated. Pre-2026 lesion studies often rely on story-based tasks, which confound ToM with language and executive demands, failing to isolate a core ToM deficit. This project directly tests the causal role of the lmFG using ecologically valid, non-verbal paradigms that align with Frith's interest in the social and experiential disruptions in schizophrenia.
proposals.hypothesis: Focal disruption (via lesion or inhibition) of the left medial frontal gyrus will cause a specific, profound impairment in the ability to represent another person's beliefs and intentions, even when assessed using non-verbal, interactive tasks that minimize general cognitive load.
proposals.method: We will recruit two participant groups: patients with focal, chronic lesions centered on the lmFG (verified by structural MRI) and neurotypical controls. All participants will complete a novel, computerized 'Interactive Tray' task, adapted from Frith's prior work, requiring real-time prediction of a partner's actions based on false belief. In a separate session with controls, we will use online, neuronavigated continuous theta-burst stimulation (cTBS) to transiently inhibit the lmFG during task performance, comparing effects to sham stimulation. Performance will be quantified using a Bayesian hierarchical model to estimate trial-by-trial belief inference accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first causal evidence for the lmFG as a necessary neural substrate for core ToM, disentangled from language. It will validate a new class of interactive, non-verbal ToM tasks for clinical populations. The findings will bridge cognitive neuroscience and psychopathology, offering a clearer model for understanding the social cognitive deficits in conditions like schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond classic verbal ToM tasks to test necessity via interactive, motor-coordination paradigms directly derived from the researcher's own methodological framework. Combining chronic lesion and acute online inhibition methods in a single study to triangulate causal evidence.

proposals.title: Causal Disruption of Medial Frontal Gyrus Function Impairs the Integration of Self and Other in Narrative Comprehension: A TMS Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Frith and others suggests the medial frontal gyrus (MFG) is crucial for mentalizing and distinguishing self-generated from other-generated actions and thoughts. Dysfunction in this region is implicated in symptoms of schizophrenia, such as delusions of control and thought intrusion, where the sense of agency is disrupted. A causal test of the MFG's role in integrating perspectives during a dynamic, ecologically valid task like story comprehension is needed to bridge cognitive neuroscience and psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transient disruption of the MFG using TMS will specifically impair participants' ability to correctly attribute narrative events or intentions to the appropriate character (self or other), leading to increased errors and altered subjective experience of the narrative, akin to a subtle, experimentally induced 'intrusion' of perspective.
proposals.method: We will adapt a narrative comprehension paradigm where participants listen to or read short stories involving two characters. Using neuronavigated, continuous theta-burst stimulation (cTBS) to temporarily inhibit the MFG, we will compare performance against sham stimulation. The primary dependent measures will be accuracy on probe questions about character intentions and source (who thought/did what), reaction times, and subjective ratings of narrative clarity and self-other blending. A control site (e.g., primary visual cortex) will be included to establish anatomical specificity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence linking MFG function to the real-time integration of self and other perspectives during naturalistic cognition. It will extend Frith's model of agency and mentalizing into the domain of complex narrative processing. The findings will offer a mechanistic framework for understanding how subtle disruptions in this neural system could contribute to symptoms of alienation and intrusion in psychiatric conditions.
proposals.novelty: While TMS has been used to study MFG in simple agency tasks, applying it during a complex, ecologically valid story comprehension task to probe the causal basis of perspective integration is novel. This approach directly tests a core theoretical link between basic cognitive neuroscience of self/other processing and the phenomenology of psychotic symptoms.

proposals.title: Neural Synchrony and Mental State Inference During Naturalistic Social Film Viewing in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Traditional theory of mind (ToM) tasks are often static and lack ecological validity, failing to capture the dynamic, multi-modal nature of real-world social cognition. This is a critical limitation for understanding the social symptoms of schizophrenia, which manifest in complex, naturalistic settings. Using naturalistic stimuli like films, which engage ToM spontaneously, offers a more direct window into the neural mechanisms of social understanding and its disruption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with schizophrenia will show reduced inter-subject neural synchrony, specifically in brain regions of the mentalizing network (e.g., TPJ, mPFC), during scenes requiring inference of characters' mental states, compared to healthy controls. Furthermore, the degree of this desynchronization will correlate with clinical ratings of negative symptoms and self-reported social cognitive difficulties.
proposals.method: We will collect fMRI data from 30 patients with schizophrenia and 30 matched controls while they watch a 15-minute edited film clip containing rich social interactions and ambiguous mental states. Using established inter-subject correlation (ISC) analysis, we will quantify neural synchrony across groups. We will employ a general linear model with regressors for time-locked 'ToM events' (identified by independent raters) to isolate activity specific to mental state inference. Complementary eye-tracking will measure gaze patterns to social cues.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, ecologically valid paradigm for studying social cognition deficits in schizophrenia, linking neural dynamics during naturalistic viewing to specific symptoms. It will advance our understanding of how the 'experiential' self is constructed during real-time social perception and how this process is disrupted in psychosis, moving beyond laboratory tasks.
proposals.novelty: While ISC has been used with naturalistic stimuli, applying it specifically to dissect ToM processing in a clinical population, and linking desynchrony to the phenomenological 'intrusion' of aberrant experiences and symptoms, represents a significant methodological and conceptual integration of Frith's prior work on motor coordination, expectation, and psychosis.

proposals.title: Explaining the Temporal Dynamics of Perceptual Experience and Social Coordination: Extending the Bag-of-Concept-Sparse-over-Representation (BoCSoR) Framework to EEG and MEG
proposals.motivation: The BoCSoR framework has shown promise in explaining complex fMRI models for visual perception, but its application to high-temporal-resolution neuroimaging is unexplored. Understanding the millisecond-scale neural dynamics underlying perceptual experience (as studied in Frith's work on ambiguous images) and inter-personal coordination (as in the 'waiter's tray' paradigm) requires methods that can explain models of EEG/MEG data. This is critical for linking computational models to the rapid neural processes associated with consciousness and symptoms in conditions like schizophrenia.
proposals.hypothesis: The BoCSoR framework can be successfully adapted to explain models trained on EEG/MEG data, revealing sparse, interpretable concepts that correspond to specific temporal components (e.g., event-related potentials or oscillatory bursts) predictive of perceptual transitions and inter-personal motor alignment.
proposals.method: We will adapt BoCSoR for time-series data by using a 1D convolutional neural network (CNN) or a transformer encoder as the base model for classifying EEG/MEG trials (e.g., seen vs. unseen percepts; high vs. low coordination). The 'Bag-of-Concepts' will be defined as learned filters or attention patterns corresponding to prototypical temporal waveforms or spectral features. Sparse dictionary learning (e.g., using a modified K-SVD algorithm) will be applied to latent representations to identify a minimal set of these temporal concepts that reconstruct model decisions, with attribution scores calculated per trial and timepoint.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first tool for generating inherently temporal, concept-based explanations for EEG/MEG predictive models, directly bridging computational psychiatry and social neuroscience. It will offer new hypotheses about the specific neural events that constitute intrusive thoughts or disrupted agency in schizophrenia, and the neural mechanisms of successful versus failed social coordination.
proposals.novelty: While XAI for neuroimaging focuses on spatial maps (fMRI) or post-hoc attribution, this proposal pioneers the extraction of *temporal* concepts from models, aligning with Frith's interest in the dynamics of experience. Applying a concept-based framework to the sub-second timescale of cognition and interaction is a novel synthesis of explainable AI and cognitive neuroscience.

proposals.title: Psychophysical modeling of social cue perception: Quantifying the precision and bias of gaze direction judgments in schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Social interaction relies on the precise, often unconscious, quantification of cues like gaze direction. In schizophrenia, social dysfunction is a core symptom, yet we lack rigorous models for how the brain's perceptual systems for social signals break down. Applying the quantitative tools of psychophysics to this domain can bridge the gap between low-level perceptual anomalies and high-level social symptoms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals with schizophrenia will show both reduced precision (higher perceptual noise) and systematic biases in judging gaze direction compared to controls, and that these psychophysical parameters will correlate with clinical measures of social withdrawal and delusions of reference.
proposals.method: We will adapt a classic psychophysical two-alternative forced-choice (2AFC) paradigm. Participants will view computer-generated faces with parametrically varied gaze angles (from direct to averted). Using a Bayesian adaptive staircase procedure (QUEST), we will estimate individual psychometric functions. Key parameters—the point of subjective equality (PSE, indicating bias) and the just-noticeable difference (JND, indicating precision)—will be derived by fitting a cumulative Gaussian function to the data. We will compare these parameters between patients with schizophrenia and matched controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal psychophysical model of a core social perception process in schizophrenia, quantifying dysfunction in terms of perceptual noise and bias. It will establish a direct, mechanistic link between aberrant predictive coding of social signals (a theoretical framework central to Frith's work) and quantifiable perceptual parameters, offering a novel biomarker for social cognitive deficits.
proposals.novelty: While psychophysics is standard in low-level vision, its rigorous application to the quantification of social cue perception in clinical populations is novel. It moves beyond questionnaire-based or coarse behavioral measures to a computational model of the perceptual disturbance itself, directly testing predictions from theories of disrupted metacognition and predictive processing in psychosis.

proposals.title: Real-World Social Context and Neural Self-Other Boundaries: An Ecological Momentary Assessment and fMRI Study in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Frith's work on motor coordination and perceptual expectations, highlights that social cognition is fundamentally shaped by real-world context. In schizophrenia, symptoms like thought intrusion and disturbances of self-experience are profoundly social, yet neuroimaging studies typically examine social processing in artificial, isolated lab settings. This creates a critical gap in understanding how daily social interactions contribute to or destabilize the neural mechanisms of self-other distinction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subjective quality and frequency of real-world social interactions, as captured via Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), will predict the integrity of neural circuits for self-other processing (e.g., TPJ, dmPFC) during a subsequent fMRI session, and that this brain-behavior link will be specifically disrupted in patients with schizophrenia.
proposals.method: We will recruit patients with schizophrenia and matched controls. For one week, participants will complete smartphone-based EMA prompts 5 times daily, rating current social context (e.g., alone/with others, sense of connection, perceived social threat). They will then undergo fMRI while performing a self/other source memory task and a mental state inference task. We will use multi-level modelling to analyze the relationship between EMA-derived social metrics (e.g., social threat variability) and neural activity/connectivity. Machine learning (e.g., support vector regression) will be used to test if EMA patterns can predict task-based neural signatures of self-other blurring.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first ecologically valid model linking moment-to-moment social experience to the neural basis of self-disturbance in schizophrenia. It will move the field beyond static lab paradigms to a dynamic, person-centered understanding of social cognitive symptoms. The method establishes a novel framework for integrating real-world experience sampling with neuroimaging in clinical populations.
proposals.novelty: While EMA and fMRI have been used separately, their tight integration to *quantify and use real-world social context as a predictor variable for neural function* in psychosis is novel. It directly tests Frith's theoretical models of self-other coordination in the ecological setting where symptoms manifest.

proposals.title: Amygdala Dynamics and Intrusive Experience: A High-Resolution fMRI Study of Aberrant Salience in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Frith's work on symptoms and self in schizophrenia, suggests aberrant salience processing—where irrelevant stimuli intrude into consciousness—is a core mechanism. The amygdala is a key hub for salience, but its rapid, dynamic interactions with cortical networks (e.g., prefrontal, anterior cingulate) are poorly characterized due to the limited temporal resolution of PET. Understanding these spatiotemporal dynamics is crucial for linking neural mechanisms to the subjective experience of intrusive thoughts and perceptions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with schizophrenia will exhibit altered, hyper-variable temporal coupling between the amygdala and prefrontal control networks during ambiguous perceptual tasks, and that the magnitude of this dyssynchrony will correlate with the subjective intensity of intrusive perceptual experiences.
proposals.method: We will employ high-temporal-resolution multiband fMRI (TR ~400ms) in patients and controls during an ambiguous image perception task (e.g., bistable figures) known to probe perceptual experience. We will use psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analysis to assess task-modulated amygdala connectivity. Crucially, we will apply dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to infer the directionality and time-varying effective connectivity within the amygdala-prefrontal-anterior cingulate circuit. Subjective reports of intrusion and clarity of experience will be collected trial-by-trial.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first fine-grained map of how amygdala dynamics contribute to the generation of aberrant salience and its intrusion into conscious experience in schizophrenia. It will bridge the gap between static neuroimaging biomarkers and the fluctuating nature of symptoms, offering a mechanistic model for how circuit-level dysrhythmia leads to specific alterations in the 'self' and perception.
proposals.novelty: While fMRI has been used to study the amygdala, applying cutting-edge high-speed acquisition and dynamic causal modeling specifically to the framework of aberrant salience and intrusive experience in schizophrenia represents a novel synthesis of Frith's cognitive neuroscience of psychosis with advanced neuroimaging techniques.

proposals.title: Intrusions of the Self: Discrepancies in Path Integration Models as a Source of Agency Disturbances in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Path integration, the ability to track self-motion to update spatial position, is a core cognitive function with established neural models in rodents (e.g., entorhinal grid cells). In humans, analogous cortical networks are implicated, but direct mapping is unclear. This gap is critical for understanding schizophrenia, where disturbances in self-monitoring and agency ('intrusions' of alien control) may stem from a faulty integration of self-generated movement signals, analogous to a path integration error at the level of conscious experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that discrepancies between rodent-derived neural models of path integration and the human cortical implementation directly map onto specific symptoms in schizophrenia, particularly the experience of alien control or intrusion, due to a failure in the subjective weighting and integration of self-generated action signals.
proposals.method: We will employ a virtual reality path integration task for healthy controls and patients with schizophrenia, measuring behavioural precision and subjective confidence. Concurrent fMRI will identify human cortical correlates of path integration (e.g., medial prefrontal, retrosplenial, and entorhinal cortex). We will then implement and compare computational models: a) a classical attractor network model derived from rodent electrophysiology, and b) a hierarchical Bayesian inference model that incorporates a 'precision-weighting' parameter for self-generated cues. Model fits to behavioural and neural data will be compared between groups, testing if symptom severity correlates with aberrant precision weighting in the Bayesian model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, mechanistic link between a well-defined animal computation (path integration) and a core human symptom (agency disturbance). It will advance the field by moving beyond simple neural homology to a formal computational comparison, offering a novel framework where symptoms arise from specific algorithmic failures in integrating predictive signals of the self.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly testing for a *computational* mismatch between animal and human models of path integration and directly linking this discrepancy to the phenomenological disturbance of self-experience in schizophrenia, rather than treating path integration only as a spatial navigation function.

proposals.title: Dissociating the Neural Correlates of Translational and Rotational Self-Motion Tracking: Implications for Agency and Psychosis
proposals.motivation: Accurate tracking of self-motion is fundamental to a coherent sense of agency and navigation. Pre-2026 research in vestibular processing and spatial cognition suggests distinct cortical pathways for processing linear (translational) and angular (rotational) motion, but their specific neural correlates during active tracking remain unclear. This dissociation is critical for understanding disturbances in agency, as seen in schizophrenia, where the 'intrusion' of unintended movements or external control may relate to a failure in integrating these distinct motion signals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tracking translational self-motion will preferentially engage a dorsal stream network including the medial superior temporal area (MST) and posterior parietal cortex, while tracking rotational self-motion will rely more heavily on the parieto-insular vestibular cortex (PIVC) and retrosplenial complex, reflecting their roles in path integration and heading updates, respectively.
proposals.method: We will use a within-subjects fMRI design where healthy participants and patients with schizophrenia track visually simulated self-motion in a virtual tunnel. Using a 2x2 factorial design (Motion Type: Translation vs. Rotation; Control: Active Tracking vs. Passive Viewing), we will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode motion type from BOLD signals. Psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses will test for condition-specific functional connectivity between key vestibular, parietal, and frontal regions implicated in agency (e.g., anterior cingulate cortex).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural dissociation of translational and rotational self-motion tracking in humans, linking specific computational demands to distinct brain networks. It will extend Frith's work on agency and motor coordination by offering a novel mechanistic framework for understanding how disruptions in these fundamental tracking systems could contribute to symptoms of passivity and control in schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has studied vestibular processing in isolation, this proposal uniquely combines active tracking tasks with MVPA to *decode* motion type from neural activity, directly testing the functional segregation of these systems and their relevance to the phenomenology of psychosis.

proposals.title: Structural and Functional Correlates of Path Integration Deficits in Schizophrenia: A Combined VBM-fMRI Study
proposals.motivation: Path integration, the ability to update one's position using self-motion cues, is a fundamental cognitive process linked to hippocampal and entorhinal function. In schizophrenia, subtle disruptions in this basic process may underlie broader anomalies in the sense of self and agency, contributing to symptoms like thought intrusion and disorganized experience. Pre-2026 work has separately identified structural abnormalities (via VBM) and functional alterations in these patient groups, but a direct, task-based link between specific grey matter volume and the neural activity supporting path integration is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with schizophrenia will exhibit specific grey matter volume reductions in medial temporal lobe structures (e.g., hippocampus, entorhinal cortex) that will correlate with both the severity of their path integration performance deficits and the aberrant functional MRI (fMRI) activity patterns in these same regions during a virtual navigation task.
proposals.method: We will recruit patients with schizophrenia and matched healthy controls. All participants will undergo a high-resolution T1-weighted structural MRI scan for VBM analysis using the DARTEL algorithm in SPM12. In a separate session, participants will perform a virtual reality path integration task during fMRI acquisition, requiring them to navigate to a remembered location without visual cues. Functional data will be analyzed using a general linear model to identify task-related activity. Crucially, we will perform multimodal correlation analyses, using individual VBM-derived grey matter volume values from regions of interest as regressors against both task performance scores and fMRI activation parameters (e.g., beta weights).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct, mechanistic link between brain structure and function in a core cognitive deficit in schizophrenia. It will move beyond simple association to show how specific anatomical alterations constrain or disrupt the neural computations of self-motion, offering a novel framework for understanding symptoms related to disrupted self-experience. The findings will inform models of embodied cognition in psychosis.
proposals.novelty: While VBM and fMRI are established, their integrated application to a specific, ecologically valid sensorimotor integration task in schizophrenia is novel. This approach directly tests the 'rigid linkage' between brain structure and dynamic function in a patient group characterized by disruptions in the coordination of internal and external experience.

proposals.title: Predictive coding of self-motion in schizophrenia: A cross-species computational model of path integration and its breakdown
proposals.motivation: Path integration, the ability to track one's position using self-motion cues, is a fundamental cognitive function linked to hippocampal and entorhinal spatial representations. In schizophrenia, subtle disruptions in the sense of self and agency, as studied by Frith, may arise from a failure to accurately integrate self-generated signals with sensory evidence. This project investigates whether computational models of rodent grid and place cell dynamics can pinpoint the specific algorithmic failures underlying spatial disorientation and 'intrusions' of aberrant experience in patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the precision-weighting of self-motion (idiothetic) cues relative to sensory (allothetic) cues is pathologically altered in schizophrenia, and that this can be formalized as a dysregulation in the gain parameters of a hierarchical predictive coding model trained on rodent neural data. This dysregulation will predict both specific neural representational errors and clinical symptoms of self-disturbance.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-existing, biologically constrained recurrent neural network (RNN) model of path integration, trained to replicate rodent grid cell formation from velocity inputs. This 'rodent-trained' model will be used as a generative model to predict human fMRI BOLD activity in the medial temporal lobe during a virtual navigation path integration task. We will fit the model to data from patients with schizophrenia and controls using Bayesian model inversion (via Dynamic Causal Modeling or similar), testing for group differences in key parameters governing cue integration and internal model updating.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, algorithmic bridge between well-characterized rodent spatial circuits and human neuropsychiatry. It will move beyond correlation to offer a computational explanation for how disruptions in fundamental neural coding schemes manifest as altered conscious experience and symptoms. It will also demonstrate a novel method for translating detailed circuit-level models to non-invasive human data.
proposals.novelty: While predictive coding has been applied to schizophrenia, using a model directly derived from and constrained by rodent electrophysiology to explain human path integration deficits is novel. It tests if a core, evolutionarily conserved algorithm for spatial self-location is a locus of pathology, directly linking Frith's work on agency to modern systems neuroscience.

proposals.title: Validating a Novel Haptic Interaction Task as a Dynamic, Objective Measure of Theory of Mind in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Theory of Mind (ToM) deficits are a core feature of schizophrenia, contributing to social dysfunction, but established tasks (e.g., false-belief, Reading the Mind in the Eyes) are often static, explicit, and susceptible to compensatory strategies. Building on Frith's work on interpersonal coordination and the 'waiter's tray' paradigm, which reveals implicit social attunement, we propose a new, objective ToM measure. Validating this against established tasks is crucial to determine if it captures a unique, ecologically valid aspect of social cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: Performance on a novel, interactive haptic coordination task (the 'rigid tray' paradigm) will correlate significantly with, but not be redundant to, performance on established explicit and implicit ToM tasks in both healthy controls and individuals with schizophrenia, suggesting it taps a distinct, action-based dimension of mentalizing.
proposals.method: We will recruit patients with schizophrenia and matched controls. The primary measure is the 'rigid tray' task, where dyads (patient-experimenter) co-transport a virtual tray using haptic robots; ToM is indexed by the patient's ability to adapt to the experimenter's unpredictable, intention-driven movements. This will be correlated with: 1) Explicit ToM (Hinting Task, Faux Pas test), 2) Implicit ToM (Animation Task, where participants describe shapes' movements in intentional vs. physical terms), and 3) clinical symptoms (PANSS). Analyses will use partial correlations and multiple regression, controlling for executive function.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial construct validation for a novel, objective, and interactive ToM paradigm. It will clarify whether impairments in schizophrenia extend to a real-time, motoric level of intention inference, potentially offering a more sensitive biomarker for social cognitive deficits and a new tool for mechanistic studies of interpersonal 'intrusion' of agency.
proposals.novelty: While animation tasks assess perceptual attribution of intent, the haptic linkage task requires a motoric *response* to another's inferred intentions in real time, creating a direct behavioral measure of interactive mentalizing not captured by passive observation or verbal response tasks.

proposals.title: Neural substrates of objective performance in a social perception task: An fMRI study of the multiple-choice animation task in schizophrenia and healthy controls
proposals.motivation: Research on social cognition in schizophrenia often focuses on subjective experience and symptom reports, but objective task performance is a crucial, dissociable component. The multiple-choice animation task, where participants interpret the social interactions of moving shapes, provides a quantifiable measure of objective social perception. Identifying the distinct neural correlates of this objective performance, separate from subjective confidence or symptom intrusion, is essential for understanding the mechanisms of social cognitive deficits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that objective accuracy on the multiple-choice animation task will be specifically associated with activity in a network involving the posterior superior temporal sulcus (pSTS) and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC), and that this association will be significantly weaker in patients with schizophrenia compared to healthy controls, independent of their subjective experiences during the task.
proposals.method: We will use functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) while 30 patients with schizophrenia and 30 matched controls complete the multiple-choice animation task. The BOLD signal will be analyzed using a general linear model (GLM) with regressors for correct vs. incorrect trials, reaction time, and subjective confidence ratings. We will employ a region-of-interest (ROI) analysis focused on the pSTS and dlPFC, followed by whole-brain exploratory analysis. Connectivity between these regions will be assessed using psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analysis to examine network integrity related to objective performance.
proposals.contribution: This study will delineate the brain systems specifically supporting accurate social perception, separating them from those related to subjective experience. It will provide a neural biomarker for objective social cognitive deficits in schizophrenia, offering a clearer target for cognitive remediation therapies. The findings will bridge Frith's prior work on motor coordination (the 'waiter's tray') and perceptual experience with the core social cognitive impairments in psychosis.
proposals.novelty: While the animation task is established, no study has yet isolated the neural correlates of its *objective* performance from confounding subjective states in schizophrenia. The design explicitly tests for a dissociation between neural activity predicting accuracy and activity related to confidence or symptom intrusion, a non-obvious distinction given the field's historical focus on aberrant experience.

proposals.title: Degraded Motion and the Attribution of Agency: Identifying Minimal Kinematic Cues for Mentalizing in Healthy Observers and Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on social cognition demonstrates that subtle biological motion cues are crucial for attributing mental states to others, a process often impaired in schizophrenia. However, the precise, minimal kinematic information required for this attribution remains undefined. Systematically degrading such motion provides a novel pathway to understand the basic perceptual building blocks of mentalizing and their potential disruption in psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, low-level kinematic features related to goal-directed movement (e.g., velocity profiles, trajectory smoothness) are both necessary and sufficient for triggering the attribution of agency and intention, and that this perceptual threshold will be elevated in individuals with schizophrenia, correlating with positive symptoms like delusions of control.
proposals.method: We will create point-light animations of simple goal-directed actions (e.g., reaching for an object). Using pre-2026 computer graphics techniques, we will systematically degrade these animations through algorithmic noise addition, temporal filtering, and spatial jittering to create a continuum of motion quality. In two experiments (healthy controls; patients with schizophrenia), participants will view these degraded animations and rate perceived agency, intention, and confidence. We will employ psychophysical reverse correlation and logistic modeling to identify the critical motion parameters driving mental state attribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantifiable link between low-level motion processing and high-level social cognition, defining a 'perceptual signature' for agency detection. It will provide a novel, computationally precise framework for characterizing the social perception deficits in schizophrenia, moving beyond broad clinical descriptions to specific mechanistic disruptions.
proposals.novelty: While previous work uses intact vs. scrambled motion, this proposal uniquely employs systematic, parametric degradation to identify a perceptual threshold for mentalizing. It directly connects Frith's prior work on agency and schizophrenia with a rigorous psychophysical approach to minimal social cues.

proposals.title: Striatal Exclusivity in Dopaminergic Reinforcement Learning: A Computational and Psychopharmacological Test in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research strongly implicates striatal dopamine in reinforcement learning, but it remains debated whether dopamine's effects are exclusive to the striatum or require functional integration with prefrontal and limbic regions for learning to manifest. Resolving this is critical for understanding the neural basis of adaptive behavior and the specific pathophysiology of schizophrenia, where aberrant dopamine signaling is linked to disrupted learning and symptom formation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dopamine's causal effect on reinforcement learning is exclusively mediated by the striatum; pharmacological modulation of dopamine will alter learning rates only when striatal function is intact, and not when striatal processing is selectively impaired or bypassed.
proposals.method: We will employ a double-blind, placebo-controlled design with a dopamine antagonist (e.g., amisulpride) in patients with schizophrenia and matched controls. Participants will perform a probabilistic reinforcement learning task during fMRI. We will use a hierarchical Bayesian reinforcement learning model (e.g., a Rescorla-Wagner variant with separate learning rates for positive/negative prediction errors) to estimate trial-by-trial learning. Crucially, we will combine psychopharmacology with dynamic causal modeling (DCM) of fMRI data to test if drug effects on behavior are mediated specifically by changes in striatal BOLD signal and its effective connectivity to other regions during prediction error computation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct test of the striatal exclusivity hypothesis, moving beyond correlation to causality. It will clarify the computational architecture of dopaminergic learning and offer a mechanistic account of learning deficits in schizophrenia, potentially distinguishing core striatal dysfunction from broader network dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: While many studies correlate striatal activity with learning, this proposal uniquely combines computational modeling, pharmaco-fMRI, and effective connectivity analysis in a clinical population to causally dissect whether dopamine's learning effects *require* the striatum, a non-obvious claim given the known widespread dopaminergic projections.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Laminar and Temporal Signatures of Distinct Prediction Errors in Human Cortex
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging studies, including those on schizophrenia and perceptual experience, often treat cortical prediction error (PE) as a unitary signal. However, this apparent unitarity may be a confound of standard-resolution fMRI, which cannot separate co-localized signals from different computational processes. This conflation limits our understanding of how distinct PEs (e.g., sensory, reward, confidence) are implemented in the cortical hierarchy, a core question for theories of hierarchical predictive coding and their breakdown in symptoms of psychosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct types of computational prediction error (sensory surprise, reward PE, confidence PE) are multiplexed within the same cortical macro-columns but are anatomically segregated into different laminar compartments (superficial vs. deep layers) and exhibit dissociable temporal dynamics, as measured by EEG.
proposals.method: We will use 7T laminar fMRI with simultaneous EEG. Participants will perform a task orthogonally manipulating sensory ambiguity, reward outcomes, and confidence reports to generate separable PE regressors. Voxel-wise encoding models will be fitted to laminar-specific BOLD signals. We will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to test for information dissociation across layers and use EEG-informed fMRI analysis to link specific temporal components (e.g., mismatch negativity, feedback-related negativity) to laminar BOLD profiles.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct, principled test of PE multiplexing versus scalar unitarity in human cortex, resolving a major theoretical ambiguity. It will offer a novel mechanistic framework for interpreting 'unitary' PE deficits in schizophrenia, potentially linking specific symptom dimensions (e.g., delusions vs. hallucinations) to dysregulation in specific laminar circuits.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used computational fMRI or EEG separately, the novel combination of high-resolution laminar imaging with simultaneous electrophysiology to decompose multiplexed PE signals is unprecedented. This directly addresses the core confound in standard neuroimaging that has obscured the neural implementation of hierarchical Bayesian inference.

proposals.title: Psychophysical Thresholds for Social Signal Detection: A Novel Paradigm Linking Perceptual Experience to Interpersonal Learning in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Social learning, a core adaptive function, relies on detecting subtle social signals from others. In conditions like schizophrenia, impairments in social cognition and agency are central, yet the basic perceptual thresholds for detecting the minimal social information needed for learning remain unquantified. This gap limits our mechanistic understanding of how disrupted perceptual experience contributes to social symptoms and maladaptive priors, a key theme in Frith's work on the 'great evolutionary mismatch' between our cognitive architecture and modern environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals with schizophrenia will exhibit elevated psychophysical thresholds for detecting social signals necessary for task learning, and that this perceptual deficit will correlate with clinical measures of self-disturbance and reduced inter-personal coordination.
proposals.method: We will adapt a two-alternative forced-choice psychophysical staircase procedure (QUEST) to measure detection thresholds for social signals. In a virtual 'waiter's tray' coordination task, the intensity of a partner's predictive movement cue (e.g., visual jerk or force feedback rigidity) will be parametrically degraded. Participants (patients with schizophrenia and controls) must detect which of two possible partner actions will occur to successfully balance the tray. Signal detection theory will analyze sensitivity (d') and criterion (c). We will also employ Frith's ambiguous image paradigm, where the 'hint' is a minimal social cue from a virtual partner, to measure thresholds for experiencing perceptual disambiguation.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first quantitative psychophysical framework for social signal detection, directly linking low-level perceptual thresholds to high-level social learning deficits. It will offer a novel biomarker for social cognitive dysfunction in schizophrenia, grounded in computational psychiatry. The work will extend Frith's models of agency and metacognition by pinpointing where in the processing hierarchy social inferences break down.
proposals.novelty: While psychophysics is classic, applying it to measure the minimal *social* signal needed for learning is novel. The approach uniquely bridges the methodological gap between Frith's work on rigid haptic linkage (motor coordination) and perceptual experience of ambiguous images (conscious access), framing both as problems of threshold-based social signal detection.

proposals.title: Synaptic Signatures of Social Learning: Distinct Neural Mechanisms for Observation and Instruction in a Rodent Model
proposals.motivation: Social learning, the ability to acquire knowledge from others, is fundamental to human cognition and disrupted in conditions like schizophrenia, where the sense of self and agency is altered. While Chris Frith's work has highlighted the high-level cognitive and phenomenological aspects of social interaction and symptom formation, the underlying cellular and synaptic mechanisms remain opaque. Bridging this gap is crucial for understanding how disruptions in basic learning circuits contribute to the aberrant experiences and beliefs seen in psychiatric disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observational learning (learning by watching) and instructional learning (learning by being told what to do) are subserved by distinct, dissociable synaptic plasticity pathways in prefrontal-striatal circuits, with instructional learning relying more heavily on prediction-error signaling via NMDA receptors.
proposals.method: We will employ a novel rodent paradigm where mice learn a simple motor sequence either by observing a demonstrator ('observation') or by receiving a predictive auditory cue ('instruction'). Using in vivo two-photon calcium imaging in transgenic mice expressing GCaMP in prefrontal pyramidal neurons and striatal medium spiny neurons, we will track population-level activity during learning. Post-learning, we will use ex vivo patch-clamp electrophysiology in brain slices to measure changes in synaptic strength, NMDA/AMPA ratios, and spine morphology in these identified circuits. Pharmacological manipulation (e.g., systemic or local infusion of the NMDA antagonist MK-801) will test the necessity of specific receptor systems for each learning type.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct cellular and synaptic comparison of observation-based versus instruction-based learning. It will link high-level cognitive theories of social learning and agency to specific neurobiological substrates. The findings will generate a mechanistic framework for understanding how disruptions in these pathways could lead to the aberrant attribution of agency and intrusive thoughts characteristic of schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: While social learning has been studied behaviorally and at the systems level, a direct, causal investigation of its synaptic foundations is lacking. This proposal uniquely integrates Frith's cognitive neuroscience framework on agency and self-monitoring with cutting-edge cellular imaging and manipulation techniques to deconstruct a core social process.

proposals.title: The Intrusion of Social Priors: Testing Predictive Coding for Social Learning in Sparse and Isolated Contexts
proposals.motivation: Social cognition is typically studied in rich, interactive settings, yet humans often learn from minimal or degraded social cues (e.g., during isolation, from sparse data). The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted how social deprivation can alter cognitive and perceptual experience. This research probes the fundamental mechanisms of social learning by examining how the brain's predictive models of others function when social input is scarce, with implications for understanding conditions like schizophrenia where social priors may be misapplied.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in minimally social contexts, the brain will exhibit a stronger reliance on prior expectations (social priors) to resolve ambiguity, leading to characteristic 'intrusions' of social content into perception and judgment, and that this effect will be altered in individuals with schizophrenia spectrum symptoms.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'waiter's tray' rigid linkage paradigm and ambiguous image perception tasks to create a 'minimally social' condition, where participants receive only intermittent, spatially degraded, or temporally delayed cues from a partner. Using computational modelling based on the Hierarchical Gaussian Filter (HGF), we will quantify the precision weighting of prior social expectations versus sensory evidence. EEG will be recorded to measure neural correlates of prediction error (e.g., the N400) under sparse social input. We will compare healthy controls with individuals reporting high levels of schizotypal symptoms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational account of how social predictive coding adapts to input scarcity, directly linking interpersonal coordination research with models of perceptual experience. It will offer a new framework for understanding atypical social cognition in psychiatric conditions not as a deficit, but as a mismatch between strong social priors and attenuated social evidence.
proposals.novelty: While predictive coding has been applied to social cognition, testing it under conditions of *minimal* social input is novel. It uniquely combines Frith's work on interpersonal motor coordination, perceptual experience, and evolutionary mismatch to study the persistence and intrusion of social models.

proposals.title: Neural Synchrony and the Experience of Fairness: A Dual-fMRI Hyperscanning Study of the Ultimatum Game in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Social interaction deficits, particularly in fairness perception and norm enforcement, are core to schizophrenia and involve a disturbed sense of self and agency. Pre-2026 hyperscanning studies in healthy participants show inter-brain synchrony during cooperation, but the neural mechanisms of disrupted interactive exchange in schizophrenia remain unexplored. This study directly tests how the 'intrusion' of aberrant priors in schizophrenia disrupts the inter-personal coordination necessary for shared social reality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during fairness exchanges, patients with schizophrenia will exhibit reduced inter-brain synchrony, specifically in prefrontal and temporoparietal regions, compared to healthy controls, and that this reduction will correlate with the severity of symptoms related to self-disturbance and the subjective experience of the interaction as intrusive or incoherent.
proposals.method: We will employ dual-fMRI hyperscanning while pairs (patient-patient, patient-control, control-control) play iterated Ultimatum Games with punishment options. Neural activity will be acquired simultaneously in two scanners linked via a dedicated interface. We will analyze inter-brain synchrony using wavelet transform coherence (WTC) and graph-based connectivity analyses on time-series data from pre-defined regions of interest (dmPFC, TPJ, AI). Behavioural data (offers, rejections, punishments) and post-scan phenomenological interviews quantifying the experience of the interaction will be coregistered with neural synchrony metrics.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first neural evidence of how disrupted predictive coding and self-modeling in schizophrenia manifest in real-time, two-brain interactions during a socio-normative task. It will link the clinical concept of 'intrusion' to a quantifiable neural marker of failed interpersonal alignment, moving beyond single-brain correlates to a dyadic model of social dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While hyperscanning has been used in healthy social cognition, applying it to the schizophrenia dyad to directly measure the failure of inter-brain coupling during fairness processing is novel. It uniquely bridges Frith's work on agency, consciousness, and motor coordination ('the waiter's tray') with the neural mechanisms of shared reality breakdown in psychosis.

proposals.title: The Temporal Dynamics of Social Cognition: Simultaneous EEG-fMRI Reveals the Neural Sequence of Fairness Evaluation and Empathic Response
proposals.motivation: Understanding the rapid, intertwined neural processes underlying social judgments and empathy is crucial for models of healthy and disordered social cognition, such as in schizophrenia where these processes may be disrupted. Pre-2026 research using fMRI or EEG alone lacks the spatiotemporal resolution to disentangle the millisecond-scale sequence of cognitive events (e.g., fairness evaluation) from their slower, distributed neural substrates (e.g., empathic response). This gap limits our mechanistic understanding of how we interpret and respond to others' social experiences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fairness evaluation in a social exchange task will be indexed by early, distinct event-related potentials (ERPs, e.g., feedback-related negativity, FRN), which will temporally precede and predict the amplitude of later hemodynamic responses in brain regions associated with empathy (e.g., anterior insula, anterior cingulate cortex) and mentalizing (e.g., temporoparietal junction).
proposals.method: We will employ simultaneous EEG-fMRI recording while participants (healthy controls and patients with schizophrenia) play a modified Ultimatum Game, observing fair/unfair offers made to a third party. EEG data will be analyzed for time-locked ERPs (FRN, P300) using established preprocessing pipelines (e.g., EEGLAB) and source localization (e.g., sLORETA). Concurrent fMRI BOLD signals will be analyzed using general linear models (GLM) in SPM12 or FSL. Crucially, trial-by-trial amplitudes of early EEG components will be used as parametric modulators in the fMRI GLM to identify brain regions where BOLD activity is predicted by the strength of the initial fairness signal.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, within-trial characterization of the temporal cascade from social evaluation to empathic engagement in the human brain. It will establish a causal-like temporal model linking specific electrophysiological markers to subsequent network-level BOLD activity. Furthermore, by comparing groups, it will identify whether temporal desynchronization in this cascade is a core feature of social cognitive dysfunction in schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: While simultaneous EEG-fMRI exists, its application to dissect the *temporal sequence* of distinct social cognitive stages within a single trial is novel. The approach moves beyond mere correlation by using early EEG signals as regressors for later BOLD activity, offering a more direct test of temporal precedence and functional linkage than previous unimodal studies.

proposals.title: Lesion Network Mapping of Necessary Nodes for Fairness-Modulated Empathy
proposals.motivation: Empathy is not a monolithic process but is modulated by social context, such as perceptions of fairness. Disruptions in this integration, where empathy is inappropriately applied regardless of fairness, may contribute to social dysfunction in disorders like schizophrenia. Pre-2026 lesion studies have identified brain regions for empathy and fairness separately, but the causal network nodes necessary for their integration remain unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fairness-modulated empathy depends on a specific, distributed brain network, and that focal lesions to key nodes within this network—particularly those involved in mental state inference and value integration—will selectively disrupt the contextual modulation of empathy by fairness, while sparing basic empathic and fairness judgments.
proposals.method: We will apply lesion network mapping (LNM) to a cohort of patients with focal brain lesions. Patients will perform a validated behavioral task where they rate their empathic concern for targets who previously behaved fairly or unfairly in an economic game. For each patient, the lesion location will be mapped to a normative connectome (using pre-2026 HCP or UK Biobank data) to identify the network of regions functionally connected to the lesion site. The necessary network for fairness-modulated empathy will be defined as the common functional network shared across patients showing the specific behavioral deficit. Statistical inference will use voxel-based lesion-symptom mapping (VLSM) and network-based statistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will causally identify the necessary neural architecture for integrating social context into empathic responses. It will provide a neurobiological model for understanding inappropriate social behavior in psychiatric conditions, moving beyond correlative fMRI evidence. The findings could inform targeted neuromodulation approaches for disorders of social cognition.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied empathy and fairness in isolation, this proposal uses a causal lesion approach to identify the integrative network, directly testing a core social cognitive function. It applies the established LNM method to a novel, context-dependent psychological construct central to Frith's work on mentalizing and its disruptions.

proposals.title: Dissociating Affective and Sensorimotor Components of Interpersonal Alignment: A Haptic Coordination Study in Psychopathy and Autism Spectrum Disorder
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research suggests empathy deficits in psychopathy and autism spectrum disorder (ASD) may involve distinct dissociations between affective sharing and sensorimotor coupling. Chris Frith's prior work on rigid haptic linkage, which promotes inter-personal motor coordination, provides a novel paradigm to probe this dissociation. Testing these clinical populations can clarify whether impaired empathy stems from a deficit in low-level sensorimotor alignment, high-level affective inference, or a specific decoupling between the two.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals with psychopathy will show intact sensorimotor coordination in a joint haptic task but a specific deficit in the affective experience of shared agency, while individuals with ASD will show impairments in the low-level sensorimotor coordination itself, leading to a cascading disruption of the shared experience.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'waiter's tray' rigid haptic linkage paradigm, where dyads (patient-control pairs) co-balance a virtual tray using force-feedback robots. Objective coordination metrics (force variance, spectral coherence) will quantify low-level sensorimotor alignment. Subjective reports of shared experience, agency, and effort will be collected using trial-by-trial ratings. The design will include conditions varying task difficulty and the presence of shared goals, allowing us to dissociate automatic coupling from strategic cooperation. We will recruit matched groups of individuals with psychopathy, ASD, and neurotypical controls.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a mechanistic, process-based account of empathy deficits, moving beyond correlational self-report measures. It will directly test theories of 'broken mirroring' in ASD versus 'cold empathy' in psychopathy. The findings will contribute to a hierarchical model of social cognition, specifying at which level of processing interpersonal alignment fails in different conditions.
proposals.novelty: While empathy deficits have been studied separately, applying a rigorous, objective motor coordination task from cognitive neuroscience directly compares the underlying mechanisms across disorders. The novelty lies in using a physically coupled interaction to simultaneously measure low-level sensorimotor performance and high-level subjective experience, a synthesis of Frith's work on motor coordination and consciousness.

proposals.title: Neural Dissociation of Affective and Sensory Components in Empathy for Pleasure: An fMRI Study in Healthy and Schizophrenia-Spectrum Populations
proposals.motivation: Research on empathy for pain has established a neural dissociation, where sharing another's negative affect recruits affective brain regions (e.g., anterior insula, anterior cingulate) but not the primary sensory cortex. It is unknown if this principle generalizes to empathy for positive, sensory-driven experiences. Understanding this boundary condition is crucial for a complete model of social cognition and may inform theories of social anhedonia and interpersonal deficits in schizophrenia, where the integration of sensory and affective signals may be disrupted.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that vicariously experiencing another person's sensory pleasure will engage core affective empathy networks but will not recruit the specific primary sensory cortices associated with the firsthand experience. This sensory-affective dissociation will be less distinct in individuals with schizophrenia-spectrum disorders, correlating with clinical measures of social anhedonia.
proposals.method: We will use a two-part fMRI paradigm. In Part 1, participants directly experience pleasant tactile stimuli (soft brush) and pleasant tastes. In Part 2, they observe videos of a confederate experiencing these same stimuli. We will employ a conjunction analysis to identify shared affective regions (e.g., ventral striatum, vmPFC) and contrast analyses to test for activation in primary somatosensory (S1) and gustatory cortices during observation versus experience. Participant groups will include healthy controls and individuals with schizophrenia-spectrum disorders. Analyses will use standard SPM12 preprocessing and general linear modeling, with psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses to examine functional connectivity between affective and sensory regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will test the generalizability of a fundamental principle of empathy beyond the pain domain. It will provide the first neural evidence for whether 'feeling with' someone's pleasure is purely affective or has a sensory component. Furthermore, by including a clinical group, it will link basic social neuroscience to the understanding of social motivational deficits in schizophrenia, potentially identifying a neural signature of disrupted shared positive experience.
proposals.novelty: While empathy for pain is well-studied, empathy for positive sensory experiences is a novel and ecologically critical test case. The direct comparison of firsthand and vicarious sensory pleasure in a multimodal design, coupled with a clinical perspective on its potential breakdown, moves beyond existing work on abstract positive empathy or facial expression perception.

proposals.title: Beyond the Tripartite Network: A Functional Connectivity and Lesion-Symptom Mapping Study of the Extended Mentalizing System in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: The dominant neurocognitive model of mentalizing (theory of mind) posits a core tripartite network involving the temporoparietal junction (TPJ), medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC), and posterior cingulate/precuneus (PCC/PC). However, this model may be incomplete, failing to account for the full spectrum of social-cognitive deficits and anomalous self-experiences in schizophrenia. A more comprehensive mapping is needed to explain symptoms like thought intrusion and disrupted sense of agency, which may rely on broader sensorimotor and subcortical integration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful mentalizing and the maintenance of a coherent self-model depend critically on the dynamic integration of the canonical tripartite network with additional regions, including the anterior insula (for interoceptive awareness), the cerebellum (for predictive coordination), and the temporopolar cortex (for social semantic integration), and that dysfunction in this extended system better predicts specific symptoms in schizophrenia.
proposals.method: We will combine multimodal neuroimaging in patients with schizophrenia and matched controls. First, task-based fMRI during a novel 'Haptic Intrusion' paradigm (adapting Frith's tray-holding coordination task to a virtual reality setting) will probe neural responses to ambiguous social-physical interactions. Second, resting-state functional connectivity will assess network integrity within the extended system. Third, we will employ multivariate lesion-symptom mapping (using voxel-based morphometry) to correlate grey matter alterations in candidate regions with clinical scores for symptoms of agency disturbance and thought intrusion. Analyses will use CONN toolbox for connectivity and SPM12 for general linear modeling and multivariate regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined neuroanatomical model of the mentalizing system, moving beyond a rigid tripartite schema. It will directly link specific components of this extended network to core phenomenological disturbances in schizophrenia, offering a more precise pathophysiological account. The findings could inform the development of targeted neuromodulation or rehabilitation strategies focusing on network integration rather than isolated nodes.
proposals.novelty: The proposal innovatively integrates Frith's work on interpersonal coordination ('the tray') and perceptual experience with the study of schizophrenia symptoms, testing the necessity of non-canonical regions for mentalizing. It directly challenges the anatomical sufficiency of the dominant tripartite model using a symptom-centric, multi-method approach.

proposals.title: The Temporal Poles as a Script Engine: A Neurocomputational Investigation of Social Knowledge Access in Mentalizing
proposals.motivation: Successful social interaction relies on mentalizing—inferring others' mental states—which is impaired in conditions like schizophrenia, leading to social dysfunction. Pre-2026 neuroimaging implicates the temporal poles (TP) in social cognition, but their specific computational role remains unclear. This project tests the hypothesis that the TP provides rapid, schematic access to social 'scripts' (e.g., a waiter holding a tray), a function critical for grounding mental state inferences in shared social knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal poles function as a hub for accessing pre-compiled social knowledge scripts, and that their neural activity patterns will specifically correlate with the retrieval and application of script-relevant information during mentalizing tasks, more so than with general semantic or episodic memory.
proposals.method: We will use a multi-method approach combining fMRI and behavioral tasks in healthy controls and patients with schizophrenia. Participants will perform a novel 'Script Inference Task' during fMRI scanning, requiring them to predict an actor's action or mental state in scenarios that either conform to or violate strong social scripts (e.g., a restaurant script). We will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode script-specific information from TP activity and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to test its effective connectivity with the core mentalizing network (e.g., TPJ, mPFC).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, neurocomputational account of how the temporal poles contribute to social cognition, moving beyond mere association. It will directly link a specific cognitive process (script access) to neural circuitry, offering a new framework for understanding the social cognitive symptoms in schizophrenia as a disruption in accessing fundamental social scripts.
proposals.novelty: While the TP's social role is known, proposing it as a dedicated 'script engine' for mentalizing is a novel functional specification. Integrating Frith's work on schizophrenia, interpersonal coordination, and perceptual expectations into a unified script-based model of TP function represents a significant theoretical advance.

proposals.title: From Dyads to Crowds: A Neurocomputational Investigation of Group Mentalizing and its Breakdown in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 social neuroscience has extensively studied mentalizing (theory of mind) in dyadic interactions, but real-world social cognition often involves attributing intentions and beliefs to groups, crowds, or collective agents. This is crucial for navigating complex social environments, from teamwork to public discourse. The evolutionary mismatch hypothesis suggests modern, large-scale societies may place unique demands on this capacity, potentially relating to symptoms of social alienation or paranoia in disorders like schizophrenia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the brain employs a distinct, scalable computational mechanism for group mentalizing that integrates perceptual cues of coordination (like the rigid haptic linkage in joint action) with higher-order social priors, and that this mechanism is impaired in schizophrenia, leading to aberrant attributions of collective intent.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel interactive task where participants (healthy controls and patients with schizophrenia) observe and interact with dynamically changing groups of virtual agents in a shared ‘tray-carrying’ paradigm, where group cohesion varies. Using model-based fMRI, we will test computational models (e.g., hierarchical Gaussian filters or particle filters) that formalize how individuals infer a group's collective goal from the coherence of individual actions. We will analyze how neural activity in the mentalizing network (TPJ, mPFC) scales with inferred group agency and compare model parameters (e.g., prior weight on collective intent) between groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal neurocomputational account of how the brain attributes mental states to collectives, moving beyond dyads. It will link basic inter-personal coordination research to higher-order social cognition and offer a new framework for understanding specific social symptoms in schizophrenia as a disruption in scaling mentalizing to complex networks.
proposals.novelty: While Frith's prior work studied dyadic coordination and hint-based perceptual expectations, this project uniquely scales mentalizing to groups using formal models and interactive paradigms, directly testing a ‘scaling hypothesis’ of social cognition impairment.

proposals.title: The Neural Correlates of Intentionality Intrusion: A Hyperscanning fMRI Study of Mental State Misattribution to Objects
proposals.motivation: Research on theory of mind (ToM) has focused on understanding others' mental states, but the systematic misattribution of mental states to non-agents (e.g., objects) is a critical, understudied inverse. This phenomenon, which we term 'intentionality intrusion,' is relevant to understanding the spectrum of human social cognition, from everyday animism to the positive symptoms of schizophrenia, such as delusions of reference where patients feel objects are communicating with them. Grounding this in pre-2026 knowledge, it bridges Frith's work on schizophrenia, agency, and inter-personal coordination with the broader cognitive neuroscience of ToM.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intentionality intrusion during a joint task with an object partner will engage core ToM neural networks (e.g., medial prefrontal cortex, temporoparietal junction) but with a distinct, attenuated pattern compared to human-human interaction, and that this pattern will be more pronounced in individuals with high schizotypy traits.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'waiter's tray' rigid haptic linkage paradigm, replacing one human participant with a programmable, object-like robotic arm. Healthy participant pairs (real human + human-robot team) will perform coordination tasks under fMRI hyperscanning. The robot's behavior will be manipulated along an 'intentionality' axis via subtle, hint-like variations in its movement path (smooth/adaptive vs. jerky/random), inspired by perceptual expectation studies. Neural data will be analyzed using dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to compare effective connectivity within the ToM network between human-human and human-object interaction conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first neural model of how the brain misattributes mental states to non-agents in a controlled, interactive setting. It will directly link fundamental social cognitive processes to clinical frameworks for understanding symptom formation in psychosis. The findings will offer a new paradigm for studying the boundaries of the social brain and the mechanisms of 'mind perception'.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has studied ToM for agents or passive observation of objects, this proposal uniquely investigates the active, reciprocal misattribution of mental states during real-time physical coordination with a non-agent, creating a controlled laboratory model of a key phenomenological intrusion.

proposals.title: The Social Brain's Domain of Application: An fMRI Investigation of Mentalizing for Animals, Robots, and Supernatural Agents
proposals.motivation: Research on the theory of mind (ToM) network, including the TPJ and mPFC, has focused almost exclusively on attributing mental states to other humans. However, humans routinely attribute intentions and experiences to non-human agents, from pets to gods. Understanding the boundaries and flexibility of this core social-cognitive system is crucial for models of social cognition and may inform clinical conditions like schizophrenia, where agency attributions can be disturbed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core ToM network will be engaged when mentalizing about animals and humanoid robots, but that mentalizing about supernatural entities will recruit additional regions associated with abstract and counter-intuitive cognition, reflecting a qualitative shift in the nature of the attribution.
proposals.method: We will adapt a well-established false-belief/animacy attribution fMRI paradigm. Participants will view short video animations of a dog, a humanoid robot, or an implied supernatural agent (e.g., a ghost) interacting with objects. Using a block design, we will contrast trials requiring mental state inference (e.g., 'What does the agent want?') with physical causality judgments. Pre-2026 standard analysis pipelines (SPM12 or FSL) will be used for whole-brain GLM analysis, with region-of-interest (ROI) analyses focused on the TPJ, mPFC, and precuneus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic neural mapping of mentalizing across a spectrum of agents, testing the domain-generality of the ToM network. It will offer a novel framework for understanding anthropomorphism and its limits, and may generate neural biomarkers for distinguishing normative from pathological agency detection, relevant to research on psychosis and delusions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied brain responses to robots or gods in isolation, no pre-2026 study has directly compared the neural substrates for mentalizing across these distinct agent categories within a single, controlled experimental paradigm, allowing a direct test of network reuse versus specialization.

proposals.title: The Cognitive Switch: Investigating the Top-Down Suppression of Mentalizing During Coordinated Joint Action
proposals.motivation: Mentalizing—the ability to infer others' mental states—is crucial for social interaction, but it can be inefficient or intrusive in contexts requiring fast, precise physical coordination, such as carrying a tray together. Pre-2026 work by Frith and others shows that such joint action can create a 'we-mode' that reduces the need for explicit mentalizing. However, the specific cognitive control mechanisms that actively *suppress* mentalizing in these contexts remain poorly understood, representing a critical gap in models of social cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful, tightly coordinated joint action triggers a top-down inhibitory signal, mediated by the right temporoparietal junction (rTPJ) and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC), which temporarily suppresses activity in the core mentalizing network (including the medial prefrontal cortex) to minimize distracting social inferences.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'waiter's tray' paradigm, where pairs coordinate to balance a virtual tray via rigid haptic linkage. In a dual-task fMRI design, participants will intermittently perform a validated mentalizing task (e.g., judging a character's belief) during coordinated versus independent movement. We will use multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode the engagement of the mentalizing network and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to test for effective connectivity from dlPFC/rTPJ to mPFC that is specifically inhibitory during coordination blocks. Subjective reports of agency and other-experience will be collected.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural evidence for an active suppression mechanism of mentalizing, moving beyond correlational descriptions. It will refine models of social cognition by specifying how the brain switches between inferential and embodied modes of interaction. The findings may also inform understanding of symptoms in schizophrenia, where a failure to appropriately modulate mentalizing could contribute to delusions of control or alien agency.
proposals.novelty: While previous work shows mentalizing is *reduced* during coordination, we test the novel, non-obvious claim that this reduction is an active, top-down inhibitory process rather than a passive byproduct of shared goals. Furthermore, we directly pit this suppression hypothesis against alternative accounts, such as mere resource depletion.

proposals.title: From Local Activation to Global Dysconnection: A Voxel-Wise Functional Connectivity Framework for Understanding Agency and Symptom Intrusion in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on schizophrenia, particularly by Frith and others, highlights disrupted sense of agency and self as core to symptoms like thought intrusion.
