While regional activation studies (e.g., in prefrontal and parietal cortices) have been informative, they cannot capture the dysfunctional large-scale network integration thought to underlie these disturbances. Scaling up from regional testing to a whole-brain connectivity analysis is crucial for a mechanistic understanding of how breakdowns in functional coordination give rise to specific anomalous experiences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific symptom dimensions in schizophrenia (e.g., delusions of control, thought intrusion) will be predicted not by altered activation in isolated regions, but by distinct, reproducible patterns of whole-brain functional dysconnectivity, particularly within networks subserving self-monitoring, salience, and cognitive control.
proposals.method: We will apply a voxel-wise, seed-based connectivity framework using general linear models (GLMs) extended to the whole brain. For each participant (patients with schizophrenia and controls), we will compute connectivity maps from thousands of seed voxels, focusing a priori on key regions from Frith's agency model (e.g., anterior cingulate, inferior parietal lobule). Connectivity matrices will be generated and analyzed using graph-theoretic measures (e.g., modularity, global efficiency) and related to symptom scores via multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) or canonical correlation analysis, as established in pre-2026 neuroimaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive, data-driven map linking whole-brain functional dysconnectivity to specific first-person symptom profiles in schizophrenia. It will move the field beyond coarse network summaries to a fine-grained, voxel-level understanding of connectivity disruptions, offering new biomarkers for symptom clusters and testing the 'dysconnection hypothesis' at an unprecedented scale within Frith's cognitive framework.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically applying a rigorous, voxel-wise GLM-based connectivity approach—typically used for focused questions—to the entire brain to deconstruct the neural basis of complex symptoms, directly translating Frith's cognitive models of agency and self into a whole-brain connectivity landscape.

proposals.title: The Bayesian Tray: A Domain-General Predictive Coding Model of Theory of Mind and Its Breakdown in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: A central debate in cognitive neuroscience is whether Theory of Mind (ToM) relies on a dedicated social module or emerges from domain-general inference systems. Resolving this is critical for understanding the nature of social cognition and its specific disruptions, such as the delusions of reference and alien control experienced in schizophrenia. Pre-2026 work on predictive coding and probabilistic reasoning provides a framework to formalize this debate computationally.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core ToM inferences (e.g., tracking beliefs and intentions) are implemented by a domain-general hierarchical predictive coding system, and that individual differences in ToM ability, particularly its pathological intrusions in schizophrenia, are better predicted by the precision-weighting of domain-general prediction errors than by the function of a putative social-specific neural substrate.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a computational model using a hierarchical Gaussian filter (HGF) to simulate belief updating in both social (e.g., false-belief tracking) and matched non-social tasks (e.g., tracking hidden states of a complex physical device). The model's key parameter is the precision (inverse variance) of prediction errors at different hierarchical levels. We will collect behavioral and fMRI data from healthy controls and patients with schizophrenia performing these tasks, fitting the HGF to individual behavior and correlating neural activity (especially in frontoparietal and temporal regions) with model-derived precision estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable computational account of ToM as domain-general inference, directly challenging modular theories. It will offer a mechanistic explanation for ToM deficits in schizophrenia as a failure of precision-weighting in a shared inference system, linking symptoms like delusions to a fundamental computational process. This bridges high-level social phenomenology with low-level neurocomputational principles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using a single, domain-general computational architecture (predictive coding/HGF) to formally equate social and non-social belief updating and in directly testing the model's explanatory power for clinical symptoms, moving beyond descriptive correlations to a mechanistic, parameter-based account of psychopathology.

proposals.title: Pharmacological Dissection of Theory of Mind: A Drift-Diffusion Model Approach to Domain-General Social Decision Making
proposals.motivation: Theory of Mind (ToM) is central to social cognition, yet its computational and neurochemical basis remains debated. Pre-2026 research shows drift-diffusion models (DDMs) successfully decompose perceptual decisions into latent cognitive processes like evidence accumulation rate and decision threshold. Applying this rigorous framework to ToM could resolve long-standing questions about whether it relies on specialized modules or domain-general decision systems, offering a new, quantitative lens for understanding social cognitive deficits in conditions like schizophrenia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reasoning about others' mental states is implemented by domain-general evidence accumulation processes. Consequently, pharmacological agents known to modulate DDM parameters in perceptual tasks (e.g., altering drift rate or threshold) will produce analogous, dissociable effects on performance and model parameters in ToM tasks.
proposals.method: We will administer classic (e.g., false belief, hinting) and novel dynamic ToM tasks requiring inference of intentions or beliefs from ambiguous social cues. In a double-blind, within-subjects design, participants will perform these tasks and matched perceptual control tasks under placebo, a dopamine agonist (e.g., levodopa, to potentially affect drift rate), and a norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (e.g., atomoxetine, to potentially affect decision threshold). Behavioral choice and reaction time data will be fit with hierarchical Bayesian DDMs to estimate trial-by-trial drift rates, thresholds, and non-decision times for each condition and drug.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, process-level test of ToM's domain-generality by quantifying shared computational mechanisms with perception. It will establish a novel paradigm for mapping specific neuromodulatory systems onto components of high-level social inference, creating a bridge between cognitive neuroscience and clinical research on social cognitive symptoms.
proposals.novelty: While DDMs have been applied to low-level decisions, their application to ToM with concurrent pharmacological manipulation is novel. This approach moves beyond correlational neuroimaging to causally test whether the same evidence accumulation machinery, modulated by the same neurotransmitters, underlies both social and non-social judgments.

proposals.title: Neural Signatures of a Hybrid Theory of Mind: Simulation Failure and Abstract Inference for Opaque Agents
proposals.motivation: The simulation-theory debate in social cognition remains unresolved, with evidence supporting both embodied simulation and abstract inference. A critical, untested question is whether these are complementary systems deployed under different conditions. This is directly relevant to understanding social cognitive deficits in conditions like schizophrenia, where the sense of agency and self-other boundaries is disrupted, and to human interactions with increasingly autonomous, non-human-like artificial agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the brain's ToM system is hybrid: it relies on sensorimotor simulation circuits (e.g., TPJ, pSTS) for predicting familiar, human-like agents, but requires recruitment of lateral prefrontal cortex (LPFC) regions associated with rule abstraction and causal reasoning when predicting agents with fundamentally opaque or 'alien' decision functions.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI while participants predict actions in a modified 'waiter's tray' coordination task and a strategic game. Agents will be: 1) a human partner (simulable), 2) a predictable algorithm (e.g., tit-for-tat), and 3) an 'adversarial' algorithm with opaque, shifting rules. Behavioral measures (accuracy, reaction time) and neural activity will be compared. We will employ multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode the representational format (simulative vs. abstract) in key regions and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to test for shifts in effective connectivity from simulation to prefrontal networks.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct neural evidence for a context-dependent hybrid ToM model, specifying the conditions that trigger a shift from simulation to abstract inference. It will offer a novel framework for understanding symptoms of schizophrenia as a failure to appropriately switch between these systems, leading to 'intrusions' of alien agency. The findings will also inform the design of human-AI interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While prior work contrasts human vs. mechanical agents, we introduce a critical 'adversarial opaque' condition that systematically breaks simulation-based prediction. This allows us to test not just *that* systems differ, but *when and why* the brain switches from one to the other, moving beyond the debate to a falsifiable conditional model.

proposals.title: Dopaminergic and Cholinergic Modulation of a Domain-Specific Self-Other Conflict Prediction Error in the Anterior Paracingulate Cortex
proposals.motivation: Chris Frith's work has established that the anterior paracingulate cortex (aPCC) is crucial for mentalizing and monitoring discrepancies between self and other, a process central to social cognition and disrupted in schizophrenia. However, the computational nature of its signal and its neurochemical basis remain unclear. This project directly investigates whether the aPCC computes a specific prediction error for self-other conflict and how this computation is modulated by dopamine and acetylcholine, key neuromodulators implicated in prediction error signaling and psychotic symptoms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate activity patterns in the aPCC specifically encode a computational prediction error signal for self-other conflict. Furthermore, we predict that dopaminergic (levodopa) and cholinergic (galantamine) challenges will differentially alter the strength and neural fidelity of this conflict prediction error decoding.
proposals.method: Healthy participants will perform a novel social belief prediction task during pharmacological fMRI (placebo, levodopa, galantamine) in a double-blind, within-subject design. A computational model will generate trial-by-trial self-other conflict prediction errors. We will apply multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) using a linear support vector machine (SVM) classifier, trained on model-derived regressors, to decode these prediction errors from aPCC BOLD activity patterns. Crucially, we will compare decoding accuracy and classifier decision values across drug conditions to assess pharmacological modulation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence for the neurochemical substrates of a core social cognitive computation, linking dopamine and acetylcholine to the monitoring of self-other boundaries. It will offer a mechanistic, model-based account of how neuromodulators might contribute to symptoms of agency disturbance and reality intrusion seen in schizophrenia, moving beyond descriptive correlations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of pharmacological fMRI with model-based MVPA to *causally* dissect the neurochemistry of a *domain-specific* prediction error, moving beyond general reward or sensory prediction errors to the social domain central to Frith's theoretical framework.

proposals.title: Conflict Monitoring in Social Cognition: Re-evaluating the Anterior Paracingulate Cortex's Role in Self-Other Distinction
proposals.motivation: The anterior paracingulate cortex (aPCC) is consistently implicated in mentalizing and theory of mind. However, its precise functional role remains debated. A reinterpretation focusing on conflict monitoring, rather than mental state representation per se, could provide a unifying account for its involvement in both social cognition and symptoms of self-disturbance in schizophrenia, such as thought intrusion.
proposals.hypothesis: The aPCC is specifically engaged in monitoring conflicts between self-generated and other-generated information, rather than in representing the content of others' mental states. This conflict signal is crucial for maintaining a coherent sense of self and agency.
proposals.method: We will employ a modified joint action paradigm (inspired by the 'waiter's tray' study) combined with fMRI. Healthy participants and patients with schizophrenia will perform a cooperative tracking task where the partner's actions are occasionally, and subtly, mismatched with their own. We will use multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to test if aPCC activity patterns classify conflict presence (self-other mismatch) but not the specific content of the partner's intended action. A computational model of prediction error will be fitted to behavioral data to quantify subjective conflict.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational and neurobiological account of a core social cognitive region, shifting the focus from representation to monitoring. It will directly link theories of agency and psychopathology, offering a mechanistic explanation for symptoms like alien control and thought intrusion in schizophrenia as failures in self-other conflict monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While the aPCC's role in mentalizing is well-established, proposing its primary function as a domain-general self-other conflict monitor is a significant departure. This integrates Frith's work on agency, schizophrenia, and inter-personal coordination into a single, testable framework.

proposals.title: An Immunological Subtraction Assay for fMRI: Isolating a Purified Theory of Mind Signal from Domain-General Cognitive Demands
proposals.motivation: A core debate in cognitive neuroscience concerns the functional specificity of the Theory of Mind (ToM) network. Standard subtraction designs (e.g., false belief vs. false photograph) are vulnerable to criticism that residual activity reflects unmatched domain-general cognitive loads (e.g., working memory, narrative complexity) rather than mentalizing per se. This ambiguity limits our understanding of the mechanisms underlying social cognition deficits in conditions like schizophrenia, where domain-general and social-specific impairments are intertwined.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by iteratively subtracting neural activity associated with a comprehensive suite of matched non-social cognitive control tasks, the residual activation within the ToM network (e.g., TPJ, mPFC) will reveal a functionally specific neural signature for mental state inference that is robust to domain-general alternative explanations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the logic of immunological absorption assays. First, we will design a primary false belief task and a hierarchy of tightly matched control tasks capturing specific non-ToM demands (e.g., tracking physical causality, remembering social facts, parsing complex syntax). Using fMRI, we will employ a multi-level general linear model (GLM) where activity from the control tasks is iteratively regressed out from the false belief task activity. The final model will yield a statistical map of 'absorbed' activity, isolating the residual signal. This analysis will be complemented by psychophysical measures of subjective experience during task performance.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a more rigorous functional decomposition of the ToM network, moving beyond binary contrasts. It will offer a novel methodological framework for isolating specific cognitive components in neuroimaging. The results will directly inform models of social cognition, clarifying which deficits in schizophrenia are specific to mentalizing versus reflecting broader cognitive 'intrusions'.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly translating the multi-step subtraction logic of immunological assays to cognitive neuroscience, moving beyond simple pairwise subtraction. This approach systematically addresses the long-standing problem of cognitive conjunction in a principled, quantitative way not previously attempted in social neuroscience.

proposals.title: Disentangling Theory of Mind: A Neurocomputational and Interpersonal Coordination Approach to Isolate Domain-Specific Activity
proposals.motivation: The brain's Theory of Mind (ToM) network is consistently engaged during mental state inference, but pre-2026 research struggles to separate its core social-cognitive function from general cognitive demands like executive control, attention, and working memory. This confound limits our understanding of social cognition deficits in conditions like schizophrenia, where symptoms such as delusions of control and thought intrusion may reflect a specific failure in representing other agents, not just a general cognitive impairment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core, domain-specific component of ToM network activity can be isolated from domain-general executive processes by combining tightly controlled mentalizing tasks with a novel, ecologically valid interpersonal motor coordination paradigm that implicitly requires modeling another's intentions.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-method fMRI design. First, a classic false-belief task will be parametrically matched for difficulty with non-social control tasks (e.g., spatial perspective-taking, working memory). Second, drawing on Frith's prior work, we will incorporate a two-person 'virtual tray' task where participants must coordinate movements to balance a simulated tray, creating a need for real-time, implicit mentalizing. We will use multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and functional connectivity (psychophysiological interaction, PPI) to identify neural patterns unique to the social conditions. Computational modeling of behavior in the coordination task will provide trial-by-trial estimates of mentalizing engagement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clearer functional map of the ToM network, distinguishing its social core from ancillary cognitive processes. It will offer a novel behavioral and neural signature of implicit mentalizing through interpersonal coordination. The findings will directly inform models of social dysfunction in schizophrenia, testing whether specific symptoms are linked to the isolated ToM component.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a tightly controlled, parametric cognitive fMRI design with a real-time, embodied interpersonal coordination task, allowing us to probe both explicit and implicit mentalizing while rigorously controlling for non-social executive demands.

proposals.title: Developmental Trajectories of the Theory of Mind Network: An fMRI Study of Functional Integration and Symptom Intrusion in Typical and At-Risk Children
proposals.motivation: Understanding the developmental emergence of the Theory of Mind (ToM) network (TPJ, mPFC, precuneus) is crucial for explaining how we represent the mental states of others, a core social cognitive ability. Pre-2026 research by Frith and others links ToM dysfunction to social impairments in schizophrenia, characterized by intrusive thoughts and a disturbed sense of self, but the normative developmental timeline of this network's functional integration remains underspecified, creating a gap in linking typical development to later psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional connectivity within the core ToM network will show a non-linear developmental trajectory, increasing with age, and that individual differences in this trajectory will predict performance on behavioral ToM tasks and parent-reported social insight. Furthermore, we predict that children at familial high-risk for psychosis will show altered developmental patterns in this network, specifically reduced TPJ-mPFC connectivity, which will correlate with subclinical symptoms of social intrusion and misattribution.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal fMRI study with 120 children aged 6-12 years (60 typical, 60 familial high-risk), scanned annually for three years. During scanning, participants will complete a well-validated animated shapes ToM task and a resting-state session. Functional connectivity between TPJ, mPFC, and precuneus will be calculated using seed-based correlation analysis (FSL's FEAT). Developmental trajectories will be modeled using mixed-effects growth curve analysis in R. We will also employ Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) on task data to investigate effective connectivity and how the influence of one ToM region on another changes with age.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed longitudinal map of the ToM network's functional development, directly linking its maturation to behavioral social cognition. It will offer a crucial normative baseline against which developmental psychopathology can be compared, specifically informing models of how early network alterations may predispose to symptoms like intrusive thoughts and reality distortion seen in schizophrenia-spectrum disorders.
proposals.novelty: While cross-sectional studies exist, this is a longitudinal design tracking the same children, allowing for true trajectory analysis. Furthermore, integrating DCM for effective connectivity with a high-risk design moves beyond describing correlation to modeling the directed developmental *dynamics* of the ToM system in relation to symptom precursors.

proposals.title: Dynamic System Perturbation of the Theory of Mind Network: A Comparative Mechanistic Study in Autism and Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Impairments in Theory of Mind (ToM) are central to the social difficulties in both autism and schizophrenia, yet the underlying neurocognitive mechanisms remain poorly differentiated. Pre-2026 research has largely relied on static fMRI localization or behavioral tasks, failing to capture the temporal dynamics of network engagement and coordination. A dynamic systems approach, inspired by methods from engineering, is needed to move beyond localization and characterize the real-time processing deficits that may differ qualitatively between these conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that autism and schizophrenia are characterized by distinct dynamic signatures of ToM network dysfunction, measurable as specific anomalies in the latency, oscillatory coupling, and damping of neural responses to graded social-cognitive load. Specifically, we predict autism will show a primary deficit in initial network engagement (latency), while schizophrenia will show unstable or prolonged resonant activity (damping).
proposals.method: We will use high-density EEG to record neural activity while participants complete a novel, parametrically graded 'Social Intention Inference' task, where visual and auditory hints progressively disambiguate an agent's intention. Time-frequency analysis and source localization will extract activity from core ToM regions (TPJ, mPFC). Dynamic integrity will be quantified using established algorithms: 1) Phase-locking value (PLV) to assess inter-regional coupling stability, 2) Event-related spectral perturbation (ERSP) to measure resonant frequency and damping, and 3) Multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode the time-course of intention representation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal comparison of ToM network dynamics across autism and schizophrenia, moving from a static 'broken module' model to a mechanistic 'dynamic processing' account. It will generate testable biomarkers (e.g., latency vs. damping deficits) that could inform differential diagnosis and the development of targeted neuromodulation interventions aimed at stabilizing network dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a controlled perturbation framework from engineering to cognitive neuroscience, treating social-cognitive tasks as a dynamic load to probe network integrity, rather than merely a behavioral output. This shifts the focus from *where* the ToM network is active to *how* it processes information over time in clinical groups.

proposals.title: Sub-second Dopamine Signaling Drives Belief-Update Failures and Theory of Mind Network Dysfunction in a Cross-Species Model of Schizophrenia Spectrum Features
proposals.motivation: Impairments in Theory of Mind (ToM), the ability to attribute mental states to others, are a core, disabling feature of schizophrenia and autism spectrum conditions. Pre-2026 research shows altered connectivity in the ToM network (e.g., TPJ, mPFC) and implicates dopamine dysfunction, but the causal, trial-level mechanisms linking fast neurochemistry to social cognitive failure and network-level pathology remain unknown. This gap prevents targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific failures in sub-second, phasic dopamine signaling in the striatum at the moment of belief updating are the proximal cause of trial-level ToM errors and the subsequent disrupted functional connectivity observed in the ToM network in clinical populations.
proposals.method: We will design analogous belief-attribution tasks for humans (fMRI) and rodents, using species-specific social cues (e.g., gaze, approach/avoidance). In transgenic or neurodevelopmental rodent models (e.g., 22q11.2 deletion), we will measure striatal dopamine dynamics with millisecond resolution using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) during task performance. Concurrently, in human patients with schizophrenia spectrum features, we will measure trial-by-trial BOLD responses in the ToM network using fMRI during a matched task. We will use dynamic causal modeling (DCM) on the human fMRI data and correlate trial outcomes with temporally precise dopamine signals from the animal models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between trial-level neurochemical events and network-level dysfunction in social cognition, moving beyond correlational human imaging. It will identify specific patterns of failed dopamine signaling (e.g., blunted or mistimed peaks) as potential therapeutic targets. The cross-species paradigm provides a validated pathway for testing novel pharmacological or circuit-based interventions aimed at normalizing information flow in the ToM network.
proposals.novelty: The proposal's novelty lies in its fusion of temporally precise neurochemical measurement from animal models with trial-resolved network imaging in humans to test a mechanistic hypothesis, rather than observing correlations in a single species. It directly tests if dopamine 'hints' or cues, akin to perceptual expectations, fail to guide belief updates, causing the clinical 'intrusion' of erroneous mental state inferences.

proposals.title: The Temporal Pole as a Social Script Engine: Disentangling Schematic Social Sequences from General Semantics via Representational Similarity Analysis
proposals.motivation: The anterior temporal lobes, particularly the temporal poles (TPs), are consistently implicated in social cognition, yet their precise functional role remains debated. Pre-2026 theories propose they act as a 'social semantic hub', but it is unclear if they process domain-general semantic knowledge or a specialized, schematic representation of social sequences ('scripts'). Resolving this is crucial for understanding the neural basis of social interaction and its disruption in conditions like schizophrenia, where social script processing may be impaired.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the temporal poles encode schematic representations of predictable social sequences (e.g., ordering in a restaurant) that are distinct from representations of matched, non-social event sequences (e.g., a mechanical assembly), even when controlling for general semantic content and complexity.
proposals.method: We will employ a 2x2 factorial fMRI design (Social vs. Non-social x Scripted vs. Scrambled sequences) using short video and narrative stimuli. Multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) will be used to test for distinct neural patterns in the TPs for social scripts. We will correlate these patterns with behavioral performance on a script inference task and include a patient lesion-deficit study (patients with TP lesions) to establish necessity. Control tasks will rigorously match linguistic, semantic, and sequential complexity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural evidence for or against the 'social script' hypothesis of TP function, moving beyond coarse lesion correlations. It will clarify whether social deficits in TP-damaged patients stem from a loss of social schemas or a general semantic impairment. The findings will refine models of the social brain and inform research on disorders of social cognition.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the adversarial experimental design that directly pits social script processing against domain-general semantic processing using RSA, combined with a lesion-deficit approach. This moves beyond simply localizing social processing to testing its specific representational format.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Control for Social Scripts: Disentangling Semantic and Narrative Knowledge in the Temporal Pole Using Comparative Methods
proposals.motivation: The anterior temporal lobes, particularly the temporal poles (TP), are consistently implicated in social cognition and Theory of Mind (ToM), but their precise computational role remains debated. A key pre-2026 question is whether the TP serves as a store of general semantic knowledge or a repository of specific social scripts and narratives. Resolving this is critical for understanding the cognitive architecture of social reasoning and its disturbances in conditions like schizophrenia, where social script intrusion or misapplication may occur.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TP activation during social reasoning reflects the retrieval of abstract social script structure, independent of general semantic feature overlap. After statistically controlling for shared semantic content using a phylogenetic comparative model, residual TP activation will correlate with the shared narrative 'ancestry' between social scenarios.
proposals.method: We will adapt phylogenetic generalized least squares (PGLS) models from evolutionary biology. First, we will construct a corpus of written social scenarios with a known, quantifiable 'phylogeny' based on shared narrative structure (e.g., betrayal, cooperation). We will also quantify their semantic feature overlap using vector space models (e.g., word2vec). Healthy participants and a clinical cohort (e.g., schizophrenia) will read these scenarios during fMRI. We will model TP activation patterns using PGLS, with the scenario phylogeny and semantic similarity matrices as predictors, to partition variance and identify neural signatures specific to script-level knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel quantitative framework to adjudicate between competing theories of TP function in social cognition. It will yield a formal method for separating script-based from semantic processing in neuroimaging data. Furthermore, by applying this to clinical populations, it may reveal whether symptoms like delusions or social misinterpretations arise from aberrant access to, or structuring of, social scripts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct importation of phylogenetic comparative methods—a robust toolkit for controlling non-independence in biology—to model the hierarchical structure of social knowledge. This approach moves beyond simple correlation of activation with task features, offering a principled way to test for distinct cognitive computations in the brain.

proposals.title: Temporal Sequencing of Theory of Mind Network Activation: An EEG/MEG Study in Schizophrenia and Healthy Controls
proposals.motivation: Theory of Mind (ToM) deficits are a core feature of schizophrenia, linked to symptoms like delusions of control and thought intrusion. While fMRI has identified a network of regions (e.g., TPJ, mPFC) involved in ToM, its poor temporal resolution obscures the dynamic sequence of neural events that underlies the construction of a mental state inference. Understanding this temporal dynamics is crucial for linking specific processing stages to clinical symptoms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful ToM requires a specific, rapid temporal sequence of activation within the ToM network, and that this sequence will be disrupted or delayed in patients with schizophrenia, correlating with the severity of specific positive symptoms related to agency and self-other boundaries.
proposals.method: We will use combined EEG and MEG to record neural activity while participants perform a false-belief animation task. Source reconstruction will be performed using pre-2026 standard pipelines (e.g., MNE-Python with FreeSurfer anatomy). We will apply time-resolved multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to track the millisecond-level flow of information between key ToM nodes. Statistical analysis will focus on time-locked differences in the onset and strength of connectivity between patient and control groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained temporal map of the ToM network in health and illness. It will identify which specific temporal stage of mental state attribution is impaired in schizophrenia, offering a mechanistic explanation for symptoms like delusions. This moves beyond localization to a process-based model of social cognition dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Frith and others established the ToM network and its dysfunction, applying high-temporal-resolution neuroimaging to explicitly sequence its activation in a clinical population is novel. It directly tests a dynamic, process-oriented model of a core cognitive deficit, moving beyond static network localization.

proposals.title: Decoding Delusional Beliefs: An fMRI-Based Decoding Approach to the Content of Pathological Beliefs in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Understanding the neural basis of delusions, a core symptom of schizophrenia, remains a major challenge in psychiatry. Current research focuses on general mechanisms like prediction error or source monitoring deficits, but cannot access the specific, idiosyncratic content of a patient's false belief. A method to decode this content from brain activity would provide an unprecedented objective window into psychotic experience, bridging the gap between subjective symptoms and neural dysfunction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the specific propositional content of a strongly held delusional belief can be distinguished from other belief contents, and from reality-congruent beliefs, based on its unique, reproducible pattern of whole-brain fMRI activity, particularly within a network involving medial prefrontal and temporal-parietal regions.
proposals.method: We will adapt multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and representational similarity analysis (RSA) techniques, using pre-trained word-embedding models (e.g., GloVe) to define a semantic space for belief content. Patients with schizophrenia and stable delusions, and matched controls, will undergo fMRI while passively reading short statements: their personal delusional belief, a matched neutral true belief, and control statements. A linear support vector machine (SVM) classifier will be trained to distinguish the neural signature of the delusional content from other contents within individuals, and RSA will test if the neural dissimilarity matrix correlates with the semantic dissimilarity matrix derived from embeddings.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide the first direct neural decoding of pathological belief content, moving beyond correlational studies. It would test whether delusions have a distinct neural representational format and offer a novel methodological framework for studying symptom-specific brain states in psychiatric disorders, with potential long-term implications for biomarker development and treatment monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While neural decoding of perceptions and simple thoughts is established, applying these techniques to decode the complex, abstract, and pathological propositional content of a delusion is novel. It directly tackles the 'inverse problem' for a core symptom of psychosis, integrating Frith's longstanding interest in the neuroscience of schizophrenia with modern multivariate decoding approaches.

proposals.title: The Suppressive Filter: A Neurocomputational Investigation of Top-Down Inhibition of Theory of Mind in Schizophrenia and Healthy Cognition
proposals.motivation: Research on Theory of Mind (ToM) has focused on its mechanisms and deficits, as seen in schizophrenia where ToM can be impaired or intrusive. However, the brain's ability to *suppress* ToM when it is irrelevant or maladaptive—a critical function for efficient social interaction and for managing symptoms like delusions of reference—remains poorly understood. This 'dual problem' of ToM control is a key gap in social neuroscience and psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that healthy individuals employ a prefrontal 'suppressive filter' to downregulate ToM network activity (e.g., TPJ, mPFC) in contextually inappropriate situations, and that this top-down inhibitory mechanism is dysfunctional in schizophrenia, leading to inappropriate mental state attributions.
proposals.method: We will use a novel fMRI task where participants observe agents performing physical actions (e.g., the 'waiter's tray' coordination task) under two conditions: one requiring mental state inference (competitive intent) and one where such inference is irrelevant or harmful (pure physical coordination). We will apply dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to quantify effective connectivity from lateral prefrontal cortex (LPFC) to the ToM network. In parallel, we will model this process using a hierarchical Bayesian inference architecture, where higher-level contextual priors from LPFC suppress the precision of ToM-related likelihoods.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first neurocomputational model of ToM suppression, identifying specific prefrontal-ToM circuit dysfunctions in schizophrenia. It will bridge Frith's classic work on schizophrenia symptoms with contemporary predictive processing frameworks, offering a mechanistic account of how context shapes social perception and its pathological intrusions.
proposals.novelty: While most studies ask how ToM is *achieved*, this proposal directly tests how it is *actively inhibited*. It uniquely combines Frith's paradigms on action coordination and perceptual expectation with a focus on suppression, moving beyond a deficit model to a dysregulation model of social cognition in psychosis.

proposals.title: Neural Dynamics of Decay: An fMRI Investigation of Phonological Store Forgetting and its Aberrations in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: The phonological store is a core component of working memory, yet the neural mechanisms underlying the passive decay of its contents over time remain poorly characterized. This is a critical gap, as subtle deficits in this basic maintenance function may underlie the cognitive fragmentation and thought intrusion symptoms observed in schizophrenia, linking a fundamental cognitive process to clinical phenomenology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rate of decay of information in the phonological store will be correlated with a specific temporal pattern of activity decay in the left supramarginal gyrus and inferior parietal lobule. Furthermore, we predict that patients with schizophrenia will show an accelerated neural decay profile, which will correlate with clinical measures of thought disorder.
proposals.method: We will use a delayed serial recall task with parametrically varied retention intervals (1-10s) during fMRI. Multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) will be applied to decode the specific phonological content being held. We will model the temporal trajectory of decoded pattern strength against behavioral recall accuracy, using exponential decay functions to quantify neural decay rates. This will be complemented by psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analysis to examine how connectivity between parietal and prefrontal regions changes over the delay.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct neural signature of phonological store decay, moving beyond simple maintenance/load effects. It will forge a novel link between a quantifiable neurocognitive deficit (accelerated decay) and the subjective experience of thought intrusion in schizophrenia, offering a mechanistic model for a core symptom.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has localized the phonological store, tracking the real-time neural correlate of forgetting *within* a trial is novel. Integrating this with Frith's longstanding interest in the neural basis of symptoms and self-experience in schizophrenia provides a unique clinical-cognitive neuroscience perspective.

proposals.title: The Developing Articulatory Loop: A Longitudinal fMRI Study of Phonological Store and Rehearsal Circuitry from Childhood to Adulthood
proposals.motivation: The articulatory loop is a core component of working memory, yet its neural development remains poorly mapped. Understanding how the phonological store and rehearsal mechanisms mature is crucial for explaining typical cognitive development and the emergence of symptoms in disorders like schizophrenia, where verbal working memory deficits and intrusive thoughts are central. Pre-2026 models, based largely on adult studies, cannot explain the developmental trajectory of these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional specialization and connectivity between key nodes of the articulatory loop—particularly the left posterior parietal cortex (phonological store) and Broca's area (rehearsal)—undergo a protracted, non-linear development, with rehearsal mechanisms showing a more prolonged maturation that correlates with improvements in working memory capacity and the subjective experience of cognitive control.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal fMRI study with children (ages 8-10), adolescents (14-16), and young adults (20-25), using a well-validated phonological similarity effect paradigm to isolate store activity and an articulatory suppression task to engage rehearsal. Data will be analyzed using a combination of univariate GLM to identify regional activation, dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to estimate effective connectivity between nodes, and multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to track the representational stability of phonological codes. This multi-method approach, grounded in pre-2026 techniques, will dissociate developmental changes in regional engagement from changes in network dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed longitudinal map of the articulatory loop's neural development, linking maturational changes in specific sub-component circuitry to behavioral performance. It will offer a neurobiological framework for understanding the development of inner speech and its potential disruption, contributing to models of both typical cognitive maturation and the pathogenesis of symptoms in schizophrenia, such as auditory verbal hallucinations and thought intrusion.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined working memory development broadly, this proposal uniquely targets the distinct sub-processes of the articulatory loop with connectivity and multivariate analyses, directly testing how their interaction matures. It integrates Frith's longstanding interest in the neural basis of symptoms and self-experience with a rigorous developmental cognitive neuroscience approach.

proposals.title: Decoding Subvocal Rehearsal: Linking Phoneme-Specific Neural Activity to Intrusive Thoughts in Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on auditory verbal hallucinations (AVHs) in schizophrenia suggests a failure to correctly attribute internally generated speech to the self. While neuroimaging studies show aberrant activity in speech production areas during AVHs, the specific content of this internal activity remains a 'black box'. Decoding the phonemic content of subvocal rehearsal from neural patterns would provide a direct, content-based neural correlate of intrusive inner speech, bridging the gap between subjective experience and brain function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate patterns of brain activity, particularly in left-hemisphere speech motor and auditory regions, can be used to reliably decode the specific phoneme being covertly rehearsed. Furthermore, we predict that the neural decodability of this content will be abnormally strong in patients with schizophrenia experiencing frequent AVHs, reflecting a failure of the mechanisms that usually attenuate the sensory consequences of self-generated thought.
proposals.method: We will use high-density EEG and concurrent fMRI in healthy controls and patients with schizophrenia. Participants will covertly rehearse specific phonemes (e.g., /ba/, /pa/) cued visually. We will apply multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA), specifically linear support vector machines (SVMs) and representational similarity analysis (RSA), to decode phoneme identity from spatiotemporal EEG patterns and fMRI BOLD signals in regions of interest (e.g., Broca's area, premotor cortex, superior temporal gyrus). The decoding models will be trained on overt articulation and tested on covert rehearsal.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel method for decoding the fine-grained content of inner speech, moving beyond coarse category decoding. For schizophrenia research, it will provide the first objective, neural measure of the specific content of intrusive subvocal activity, directly testing theories of defective corollary discharge and self-monitoring. This could lead to new biomarkers for symptom severity and novel targets for cognitive therapies.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have decoded broad categories of imagined stimuli, decoding specific, rehearsed phonemes from non-invasive neural data in a clinical population is a significant technical and conceptual advance. It directly applies the 'inverse' decoding approach to the core phenomenological disturbance in schizophrenia—intrusive thoughts—linking Frith's seminal cognitive model to contemporary neural decoding techniques.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Simulation-Theory Account: Testing the Necessity of Abstract Inference for Opaque Minds
ideas.description: This research program tests the hypothesis that Theory of Mind employs simulation for familiar agents but requires abstract, theory-like inference for agents with decision functions fundamentally different from our own. Using fMRI and behavioral tasks, participants predict the actions of both human-like and algorithmically 'alien' agents. The design isolates when the brain's simulation circuits fail and when higher-order prefrontal regions associated with rule abstraction and causal reasoning become necessary for accurate prediction, providing a falsifiable model of a hybrid ToM system.

ideas.title: Disentangling Social Scripts from General Semantics in the Temporal Poles via Representational Similarity
ideas.description: This project aims to isolate the specific contribution of the temporal poles to social cognition by testing whether they encode schematic social sequences (scripts) independently of general semantic knowledge. It uses a factorial fMRI design with carefully matched social and non-social sequential events, analyzed for neural pattern distinctiveness. The design incorporates lesion-deficit correlations and rigorous control tasks to rule out domain-general semantic explanations, providing a direct test of the social script hypothesis.

ideas.title: Pharmacological Decoding of Self-Other Conflict Prediction Errors in the Anterior Paracingulate
ideas.description: This research program combines pharmacological fMRI with model-based multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to test the hypothesis that the anterior paracingulate cortex (aPCC) computes a domain-specific prediction error signal for self-other conflict. Participants will perform a social belief prediction task during fMRI under different pharmacological challenges. Trial-by-trial model-derived conflict prediction errors will be used to train classifiers on fMRI data, specifically testing if aPCC activity patterns decode these signals. By observing how different neuromodulators alter the strength and fidelity of this decoding, the study can causally dissect the neurochemical basis of monitoring discrepancies between one's own and others' perspectives.

ideas.title: Assessing ToM Network Integrity via Dynamic Systems Analysis and Targeted Stimulation
ideas.description: This research seed proposes examining the functional integrity of the Theory of Mind (ToM) network in autism and schizophrenia. Drawing an analogy from structural engineering, where the integrity of complex structures is tested by applying controlled vibrational loads and analyzing the dynamic response, this idea adapts that solution to cognitive neuroscience. The adapted research idea conceptualizes the ToM network as a dynamic system and proposes assessing its integrity by using graded, controlled social-cognitive tasks as a 'load' while measuring the network's response with high-temporal-resolution tools like EEG or MEG. This method aims to move beyond static functional localization to identify specific dynamic processing deficits—such as abnormal latency, resonant frequency, or damping—that differentiate the neurocognitive mechanisms of ToM impairment in each clinical population, potentially revealing whether the dysfunction stems from a failure to engage the network, unstable dynamics, or inefficient information propagation.

ideas.title: Drift-Diffusion Modeling of Theory of Mind as Domain-General Evidence Accumulation
ideas.description: This research program proposes that reasoning about others' mental states (Theory of Mind) is not the product of a specialized module but emerges from domain-general evidence accumulation processes, similar to perceptual decisions. We test this by applying drift-diffusion models (DDMs) to choice behavior in classic and novel ToM tasks, quantifying parameters like evidence accumulation rate (drift) and decision threshold. Crucially, by administering pharmacological agents, we can experimentally manipulate these DDM parameters during ToM reasoning, dissociating specific cognitive components (e.g., evidence sensitivity from caution) and directly comparing their modulation to effects seen in perceptual control tasks. This approach provides a rigorous, process-level test of ToM's domain-generality and offers a new paradigm for understanding how neuromodulators alter high-level social cognition.

ideas.title: Adapting Immunological Subtraction to Isolate ToM-Specific Neural Activity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a classic problem in immunology—isolating a specific antibody's activity from a serum containing many antibodies—to the challenge of isolating Theory of Mind (ToM)-specific brain activity from general cognitive demands. In immunology, 'absorption assays' remove cross-reactive antibodies by pre-incubation with non-target antigens, leaving only the target-specific antibodies. Adapted to cognitive neuroscience, the method involves designing a suite of tightly matched control tasks that individually and collectively capture the non-ToM cognitive loads (e.g., working memory for social facts, attention to agent movement, narrative comprehension) present in a primary ToM task (e.g., interpreting a character's false belief). Using fMRI, the neural activity from these control tasks is iteratively 'subtracted' from the activity in the ToM task. The resulting residual activation map within the canonical ToM network would represent a more purified signal of mental state inference, helping to test its functional specificity against domain-general alternatives.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Comparative Methods to Disentangle Social Scripts from Semantic Knowledge in Temporal Pole Function
ideas.description: This idea adapts phylogenetic comparative methods from evolutionary biology to clarify the role of the temporal poles in Theory of Mind (ToM). The analogous problem in phylogenetics is distinguishing whether trait correlations are due to shared ancestry or independent functional links. The analogous solution uses statistical models on trait data across an evolutionary tree to control for phylogenetic non-independence. Adapted to neuroscience, we design social reasoning tasks where scenarios are modeled as having a 'phylogeny' based on shared narrative or script structure. Participants engage with these scenarios during fMRI. By analyzing temporal pole activation patterns with models that account for the shared script 'ancestry' between scenarios, we can dissociate activation related to retrieving general semantic knowledge (which should correlate with feature overlap) from activation specific to accessing abstract social script knowledge (which should correlate with the script phylogeny after controlling for semantics). This provides a novel methodological framework to test if temporal poles provide social scripts or semantic knowledge for ToM.

ideas.title: A Predictive, Domain-General Computational Model of Theory of Mind
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond a theoretical challenge to propose and test a concrete, domain-general computational model of Theory of Mind (ToM). It posits that core ToM inferences (e.g., about beliefs and intentions) are performed by domain-general probabilistic reasoning systems, not a dedicated social module. The program tests this by predicting and verifying strong neural and behavioral overlap between social reasoning and analogous non-social reasoning tasks. Success would be demonstrated by showing that individual differences in domain-general executive function and probabilistic reasoning predict ToM ability better than measures of a putative social-specific module.

ideas.title: Linking sub-second dopamine signaling to trial-level ToM network dysfunction in clinical models
ideas.description: This research program integrates human clinical neuroimaging with animal model neurochemistry to establish a mechanistic link between trial-by-trial social cognition and neuropathology. We will design analogous belief-attribution tasks for humans (fMRI) and rodents (leveraging species-specific social cues), measuring whole-brain Theory of Mind (ToM) network activity and sub-second striatal dopamine dynamics via fast-scan cyclic voltammetry, respectively. By comparing these temporally precise measures in transgenic or neurodevelopmental rodent models of autism and schizophrenia spectrum features, we can test if specific patterns of failed dopamine signaling at key decision points (e.g., belief update) are the proximal cause of the disrupted network connectivity seen in patients. This approach moves beyond correlation to identify potential neurochemical targets for normalizing information flow in the clinically impaired ToM network.

ideas.title: Laminar and temporal decomposition of prediction error multiplexing in human cortex
ideas.description: This study uses ultra-high-field 7T laminar fMRI combined with simultaneous EEG to test whether the apparent unitarity of prediction error signals in standard neuroimaging is an artifact of insufficient measurement resolution. By designing tasks that orthogonally manipulate sensory surprise, reward prediction error, and confidence prediction error, and fitting voxel-wise encoding models with separate computational regressors for each PE type, we can assess whether distinct PE components map onto different cortical layers and temporal dynamics within the same region. Multivariate pattern analysis across laminar compartments will determine if superficial and deep layers carry dissociable PE information, as predicted by hierarchical predictive coding theories. This approach directly overcomes the confound that a single BOLD measure at standard resolution conflates multiple co-localized signals, providing the first principled test of PE multiplexing versus true scalar unitarity in human cortex.

ideas.title: Mapping Dynamic Theory of Mind Network Interactions with High-Density Electrophysiology and Granger Causality
ideas.description: This idea proposes to study connectivity within the Theory of Mind (ToM) network by drawing an analogy from Systems Ecology, where scientists decipher complex, dynamic interactions in food webs using high-frequency sensor data and causal inference techniques. The analogous solution involves deploying dense sensor arrays (e.g., for tracking animals or environmental DNA) and using methods like Convergent Cross Mapping to infer directional influences and dynamic couplings, moving beyond simple correlation. Adapted to neuroscience, this translates to using high-density EEG or MEG to obtain millisecond-resolution electrophysiological data from the core ToM regions. Subsequently, time-series causality methods, such as spectral Granger causality, would be applied to this data to model the directed, frequency-specific information flow between network nodes during task performance. This approach moves beyond fMRI's slow hemodynamic signals to characterize the rapid, directional interactions that likely underpin social reasoning.

ideas.title: Theory of Mind as an Emergent Property of Domain-General Predictive Processing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that Theory of Mind (ToM) is a distinct cognitive module by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's critique of pan-adaptationism. In biology, Gould and Lewontin's 'spandrel' concept argued that many traits are not separate adaptations but emergent by-products of general architectural constraints. Adapting this solution, the research proposes that ToM is not a unified, domain-specific module but rather an emergent property of domain-general predictive processing and social reasoning systems. The adapted research would seek evidence that ToM abilities are a natural consequence of these broader cognitive architectures, challenging the need to posit a separate, dedicated ToM module.

ideas.title: Inferring Dopaminergic Tone via Behavioral 'Seismology'
ideas.description: This research adapts the core inverse problem from geophysics—inferring unseen internal structure from surface observations—to computational neuroscience. In seismology, the interior of the Earth is inferred from seismic waves. Here, the 'interior' is an individual's latent dopaminergic tone, and the 'surface waves' are their observable behavioral choice data in decision-making tasks. The adapted solution is to construct a detailed generative model that maps parameters representing baseline dopamine levels, phasic response magnitudes, and learning rates to probability distributions over choice sequences. By applying inverse methods (e.g., Bayesian inference, maximum likelihood estimation) to fit this model to behavioral data, we can solve for the dopaminergic parameters, effectively using behavior as a non-invasive 'seismograph' for dopaminergic function.

ideas.title: Apply lesion-symptom mapping to ToM using a multi-symptom network inference approach
ideas.description: This idea expands the original lesion-symptom mapping concept for Theory of Mind (ToM) by drawing an analogy from ecosystem ecology. In ecology, the necessity of a species is inferred by analyzing network relationships in naturally disturbed systems, not just single knock-outs. The analogous solution uses structural equation modeling on observational disturbance data to model a species' causal role across multiple ecosystem functions. Adapted to neuroscience, the research would use focal brain lesion data to construct a causal network model linking three candidate regions to multiple, dissociable ToM sub-functions. By applying causal inference techniques (e.g., SEM) to the lesion data, the study can test the specific necessity of each region while modeling how its loss propagates through the cognitive network, offering a more nuanced test of necessity than simple lesion-deficit correlations.

ideas.title: Dual: Study serotonin's interaction with dopamine using a network control theory framework
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from Electrical Engineering and Network Control Theory. The analogous problem involves controlling a complex network (like a power grid) using dual-input strategies to efficiently steer the system. The analogous solution uses control theory to model input propagation and identify key driver nodes. Adapted to neuroscience, the idea is to formally model the serotonin and dopamine systems as interacting control networks. The study would investigate how serotonergic manipulation acts as a secondary control input to the dopaminergic prediction error network. Using computational modeling and possibly targeted pharmacological fMRI or fiber photometry, the research aims to identify how 5-HT activity at specific projection sites modulates the gain and temporal dynamics of DA signals, thereby providing a mechanistic account of how serotonin shapes reinforcement learning and adaptive behavior.

ideas.description: This research adapts the longitudinal, systems-based approach from ecosystem ecology (exemplified by Long-Term Ecological Research sites) to study the development of the Theory of Mind (ToM) network. The adapted study would use longitudinal fMRI in children to map the ToM network (mPFC, TPJ, precuneus) not just as isolated regions, but as a dynamic system. By applying network analysis and dynamic causal modeling across development, the research aims to characterize the system's trajectory, identify critical transition points in functional connectivity, and model how a stable, mature ToM network architecture emerges from earlier, more variable states.

ideas.title: Decomposing prediction error signals via spectral unmixing: Lessons from remote sensing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the BOLD prediction error signal is unitary and scalar by drawing an analogy to spectral unmixing in remote sensing. In hyperspectral imaging, each pixel captures a composite spectral signature from multiple ground materials, yet unmixing algorithms successfully decompose these into pure endmember spectra and their proportional contributions. Similarly, the BOLD signal within a given brain region likely reflects a superposition of multiple prediction error signals—reward prediction errors driven by dopamine, sensory precision errors modulated by acetylcholine, and state prediction errors linked to serotonin—that are conflated into a single hemodynamic measurement. The adapted approach would involve: (1) defining computational endmembers by constructing model-derived regressors for each hypothesized prediction error type using reinforcement learning and predictive coding frameworks; (2) designing multi-task fMRI paradigms that differentially engage these error types to provide the spectral contrast needed for unmixing; and (3) applying linear unmixing and independent component analysis methods to the voxelwise BOLD time series to estimate the fractional abundance of each prediction error component. This would move the field beyond treating prediction error as a monolithic signal and toward a quantitative decomposition of its constituent computational and neurochemical sources.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismology's Array Processing to Isolate Trial-Specific Neurotransmitter Signals
ideas.description: The original idea aims to measure trial-by-trial neurotransmitter dynamics using fast-scan cyclic voltammetry (FSCV) in animals during tasks analogous to human experiments. The core challenge is isolating the fast, weak signal of a single cognitive/behavioral trial from overlapping neurochemical noise. Drawing an analogy from seismology, where arrays of sensors and beamforming techniques are used to isolate specific earthquake signals from continuous background seismic noise, we propose adapting this solution. By implanting a dense micro-array of FSCV sensors in the relevant brain region and applying adapted array-processing algorithms, we can computationally 'steer' the measurement focus to enhance the transient dopamine release associated with a specific trial. This allows us to deconvolve overlapping trial signals and achieve a cleaner, trial-specific readout of sub-second dopamine dynamics, effectively scaling down the measurement from block averages to discrete events.

ideas.title: Identifiability-Constrained Joint Inference of Neuromodulatory States from Multi-Paradigm Behavior and Sparse Biomarkers
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea addresses the fatal identifiability problem in inferring dopamine from choices by developing a computational framework that jointly estimates a manifold of latent neuromodulatory and cognitive states. It employs a series of targeted behavioral tasks optimized to dissociate the behavioral signatures of dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin systems based on pre-2026 computational psychiatry literature. The model is disciplined and validated by incorporating minimal, scalable physiological measures (e.g., task-evoked pupillary responses, heart rate variability) as occasional anchoring points within a Bayesian system identification pipeline, enabling testable, causally richer estimates of individual neurochemical tone that are robust to confounding variables.

ideas.title: Tracer-Kinetic Dual-Modality Imaging for Neurotransmitter Dynamics During Learning, Inspired by Dynamic Contrast-Enhanced MRI in Oncology
ideas.description: In oncology, the challenge of distinguishing true drug-receptor engagement from nonspecific tissue effects was solved using dual-modality imaging: DCE-MRI captures hemodynamic changes while PET quantifies receptor occupancy, unified by pharmacokinetic compartmental models that decompose tracer signals into bound and unbound fractions over time. We adapt this approach to cognitive neuroscience by applying analogous compartmental kinetic models to simultaneous PET-fMRI acquisitions during reinforcement learning tasks. Specifically, a displaceable radioligand such as [11C]raclopride is administered during continuous PET acquisition, and a linearized simplified reference tissue model—paralleling the Tofts compartmental framework—is used to estimate time-varying dopamine release and receptor occupancy at temporal resolutions approaching individual learning trials. Concurrently acquired fMRI BOLD signals provide trial-locked prediction error estimates from regions like the ventral striatum. By regressing the PET-derived dopamine occupancy time course against the fMRI prediction error regressors within a unified statistical framework, one can directly test whether momentary fluctuations in dopamine receptor binding predict the magnitude and sign of neural prediction error signals—moving beyond correlational approaches to a mechanistically grounded, pharmacokinetically validated link between neurochemistry and computation during learning.

Behavioral and neural data are analyzed through separate computational models to quantify parameters linked to each population's proposed dopaminergic pathophysiology. The design includes controlled pharmacological manipulations within each patient group to establish a direct causal link between the targeted dopaminergic system and the observed behavioral effects, ensuring clinically interpretable results.

ideas.title: Adapting a 'Common Protocol' Approach from Ecology to Scale Clinical Dopamine Research
ideas.description: The original idea of scaling a paradigm to clinical populations with dopamine dysfunction (e.g., Parkinson's, schizophrenia) faces the challenge of heterogeneity in symptoms, medication, and disease stages, making direct comparison difficult. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where scientists use standardized, simplified measurement protocols (like Essential Biodiversity Variables) to collect comparable data across diverse global ecosystems, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea is to develop a minimal, standardized experimental 'kit'—a core set of validated, low-burden cognitive/behavioral tasks and physiological measures (e.g., specific reinforcement learning tasks, pupil dilation, simple motor assays) that are robustly linked to dopamine function. This kit would be deployed across multiple clinical sites studying different dopamine-related disorders and healthy controls. By using this common protocol, data can be aggregated to map transdiagnostic gradients of dopamine dysfunction, directly testing the clinical relevance and generalizability of the original paradigm across the spectrum of dopamine pathophysiology.

ideas.title: Adapting speech decoding pipelines from BCI research to decode pharmacologically-modulated prediction errors from fMRI
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between decoding intended speech from cortical recordings in BCI research and decoding computational prediction errors from pharmacological fMRI. In the BCI domain, researchers faced the challenge of extracting a latent cognitive variable (intended speech) from noisy, high-dimensional neural population activity. They solved this with multi-stage pipelines: first reducing dimensionality of neural patterns, then temporally aligning variable neural dynamics to output sequences, and finally applying probabilistic decoding constrained by language-model priors. The pharmacological fMRI decoding problem is structurally parallel: we must extract a latent computational variable (trial-by-trial prediction error) from noisy, high-dimensional BOLD signals that are further confounded by drug-induced vascular and neurochemical changes. Adapting the BCI approach, we propose a pipeline that (1) applies multivariate pattern analysis to reduce voxel-space fMRI data into informative neural state representations, (2) uses temporal alignment methods to handle the variable and drug-altered hemodynamic response function, and (3) employs a probabilistic decoder whose structural prior is not a language model but a reinforcement learning model's prediction error time series. This computational-model prior constrains the decoder to solutions consistent with learning theory, improving signal separation between genuine prediction error encoding and pharmacokinetic confounds. The approach would enable researchers to ask precisely how dopaminergic or serotonergic manipulations alter the neural encoding of prediction errors at the single-trial level.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Trophic Network Analysis to Map Dopamine Dynamics in Multi-Agent Games
ideas.description: To scale up testing of the dopamine-prediction error link to complex, multi-step social or economic games, we draw an analogy from ecology. Ecologists solve the problem of analyzing complex, interdependent systems (like food webs) by using network models to map flows of energy and identify critical nodes and stability properties. We adapt this solution by conceptualizing the strategic game as an interaction network. Agents' dopamine prediction error signals, measured during gameplay, are quantified as flows across the network edges formed by their social and economic choices. This framework allows us to use established network analysis tools (e.g., calculating centrality, community structure, and cascade effects) to systematically investigate how prediction errors propagate and cluster within complex strategic environments, moving beyond simple dyadic or low-step paradigms.

ideas.title: Perfusion-corrected multivariate decoding of pharmacologically modulated prediction errors in fMRI
ideas.description: This approach applies multivariate pattern analysis to decode trial-by-trial prediction errors from fMRI data under pharmacological challenge, while using simultaneous dual-echo ASL-BOLD acquisition to measure and correct for drug-induced changes in cerebral perfusion and neurovascular coupling. A within-subject placebo-controlled crossover design enables training pattern classifiers on drug-free sessions and testing their generalization to pharmacological sessions, ensuring that decoded signals reflect genuine computational variables rather than hemodynamic artifacts. Decoded prediction-error patterns are further validated through formal computational model comparison, contrasting prediction-error regressors derived from reinforcement learning models against alternative decision variables such as unsigned surprise and chosen value. This multi-layered validation strategy overcomes the central confound of pharmacological fMRI—that drugs alter the hemodynamic response itself—yielding neurally specific readouts of how neuromodulatory systems shape learning computations.

ideas.title: Adapting Computational Neuroscience's Attractor Network Models to Decompose Drug Effects on Choice Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea transfers the analytical framework used in computational ecology's optimal foraging theory to pharmacological decision-making research. In foraging ecology, sequential sampling models decompose an animal's patch-leaving decisions into distinct cognitive components: the rate of evidence accumulation about resource quality, the decision threshold governing when to act, and non-decision time reflecting movement constraints. This decomposition allowed ecologists to pinpoint whether environmental changes affected perception versus policy. Adapting this approach, we apply hierarchical drift-diffusion models (HDDMs) to drug choice behavior in both preclinical and clinical settings. In a concurrent drug self-administration paradigm, subjects make repeated choices between drug and alternative reinforcers. By fitting HDDMs to the full reaction-time distributions and choice data, we extract three key parameters per condition: drift rate (reflecting the relative valuation signal favoring drug over alternative), boundary separation (reflecting response caution or impulsivity in committing to a choice), and non-decision time (capturing pharmacokinetic or motor effects). Critically, the hierarchical Bayesian estimation allows individual-level parameter recovery while borrowing strength across subjects. This decomposition reveals whether a pharmacological manipulation (e.g., a candidate therapeutic) reduces drug choice by attenuating the drug's subjective value signal (lower drift toward drug), by restoring deliberative control (wider boundaries), or merely by slowing motor execution. The ecological analogy further suggests incorporating time-varying drift rates to capture within-session satiation effects, paralleling how foraging models handle depleting patches, enabling detection of how drugs alter the temporal dynamics of value updating across a self-administration session.

ideas.title: Dissociating modulatory roles via causal perturbation: Lessons from auxin signaling in plant biology
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from plant developmental biology, where the hormone auxin was historically reduced to a single role (growth promotion) until targeted genetic and molecular tools revealed it simultaneously controls multiple independent processes—cell elongation, directional growth, and tissue patterning—through distinct receptor cascades (TIR1/AFB for transcriptional regulation vs. ABP1 for rapid membrane responses). The key methodological insight was using tissue-specific and receptor-specific perturbations to dissociate functions that had been confounded when studying the whole-organism phenotype. Adapting this approach to the dopamine debate: rather than inferring dopamine's role from broad pharmacological manipulations or global knockout studies (which conflate learning, motivation, and vigor), researchers should deploy projection-specific optogenetic or chemogenetic tools to independently manipulate dopamine signaling in distinct circuits—ventral tegmental area to nucleus accumbens (motivation/reward valuation), substantia nigra to dorsal striatum (vigor/movement invigoration), and phasic VTA burst firing (reward prediction error/learning). By selectively restoring or silencing dopamine in each pathway while measuring choice behavior, one can determine whether the observed influence of dopamine on decision-making is truly reducible to learning alone or instead reflects a composite of separable motivational, energizing, and learning signals channeled through parallel receptor and circuit mechanisms. This circuit-dissociation framework directly challenges the assumption that dopamine's effect on choice operates exclusively through a learning mechanism.

ideas.title: Computational taxonomy of individual learning algorithms via Bayesian model comparison with recovery validation
ideas.description: This project directly tests whether a single action-value algorithm suffices to explain individual differences in learning by fitting a structured family of candidate algorithms—including model-free, model-based, hybrid, and meta-learning architectures—to individual participant data using hierarchical Bayesian model comparison. Simulation-based model recovery analysis first establishes that the experimental tasks and fitting procedures can reliably distinguish between algorithmic classes, not merely parameter regimes. The single-algorithm hypothesis is formally embedded as a nested model, allowing principled adjudication of whether observed behavioral variation reflects parametric differences within one algorithm or genuine algorithmic heterogeneity across individuals. This adversarial design ensures that both the single-algorithm and multi-algorithm accounts receive a fair test, overcoming the confirmation bias inherent in assuming algorithmic uniformity from the outset.
