ideas.title: Mixture-of-Strategies Models for Learning: Borrowing from Ecological Niche Partitioning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the common assumption in computational modeling of decision-making that a single action-value algorithm (varying only in fitted parameters) adequately describes all subjects. The analogy is drawn from community ecology, where the parallel assumption—that all species use a single foraging strategy—was overturned by niche partitioning theory. Ecologists found that coexisting species employ qualitatively distinct resource-exploitation strategies, identified through mixture models and clustering of behavioral data. Adapting this solution, we propose applying latent-mixture modeling and Bayesian model comparison to behavioral datasets from reinforcement learning tasks. Instead of fitting one algorithm (e.g., Q-learning) to all participants and attributing differences solely to parameters like learning rate or softmax temperature, we would test whether the population is better described by a mixture of qualitatively distinct strategy classes—for example, model-free learners, model-based planners, heuristic users, and directed explorers. This ecological lens reframes individual differences not as noise around a single algorithm but as a structured taxonomy of cognitive strategies, each potentially adaptive in different environmental contexts, much as ecological niches support distinct foraging phenotypes.

ideas.title: Pharmacokinetically-linked time-varying drift-diffusion models for dissecting drug effects on decision processes
ideas.description: This approach extends drift-diffusion modeling of drug-affected choice behavior by embedding pharmacokinetic time courses directly into the decision model, allowing drift rate, boundary separation, and non-decision time to vary as parametric functions of estimated drug concentration rather than being treated as static parameters. A hierarchical Bayesian framework captures individual differences in drug sensitivity and metabolic rate while providing principled uncertainty quantification and regularization across sparse within-subject data. Formal model comparison between stationary and pharmacokinetically-linked non-stationary variants quantifies the inferential cost of ignoring temporal dynamics, addressing the critical identifiability problem that undermines standard DDM applications in pharmacological research. The framework supports multi-dose within-subject designs and can dissect whether a drug primarily affects evidence quality, response caution, or motor execution at each phase of drug action.

ideas.title: Continuous glucose monitoring paradigm applied to real-time neurochemical sensing in humans
ideas.description: This idea adapts the successful translation pathway of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) technology to the challenge of measuring extracellular dopamine in humans during learning. In diabetes care, the field moved from invasive laboratory blood glucose assays to minimally invasive, real-time subcutaneous electrochemical sensors by miniaturizing sensor geometry, developing biocompatible surface coatings, implementing wireless data transmission, and creating robust calibration algorithms that correct for sensor drift and tissue interference. Applying this paradigm to dopamine measurement, researchers would develop thin-film electrochemical microprobes functionalized with dopamine-selective recognition elements (such as engineered aptamers or tyrosinase-based enzyme layers) housed in biocompatible polymer sheaths. These probes, far smaller than conventional microdialysis cannulae, would be implanted in target regions accessible during neurosurgical procedures (e.g., in patients undergoing deep brain stimulation electrode placement) and connected to miniaturized wireless potentiostats. Signal processing algorithms adapted from CGM drift-correction methods would compensate for biofouling and interferent signals in the brain's extracellular milieu. This approach could ultimately yield continuous dopamine concentration profiles with sub-second temporal resolution during cognitive learning paradigms, bridging the gap between animal microdialysis findings and human neurochemistry with a clinically translatable platform.
